Term used in the glass trade for a type of art glass developed by the Mt. Washington Glass Company, shaded from pink to yellow with pink at the top caused by the presence of gold and uranium.

Lacquer obtained from the sap of the Melanorrhoea (Gluta) usitata tree, which belongs to the Anacardiaceae family and grows in Laos, Myanmar, Thailand and Cambodia. The sap is harvested from the tree by tapping and has a straw colour, which quickly turns glossy black. Its main function is to waterproof and heatproof the object and has excellent adhesive properties, as well as good stability in hot, acid and alkaline conditions.
A fabricated wood product produced in the 1920s by the Italian paper industry as a decorative coating and finishing material used mainly for interiors and furnishings, especially intarsia work.
A fine-grained French oolitic limestone that is cream in color and easily carved.
Pigment used in the 19th century, composed of lead chlorosulfite.
Wood of the tree belonging to the genus Quercus kelloggii, the bark of which is a source of tannin.
Wood from the species Aesculus californica.
Tough, coarse-grained wood of the species Platanus racemosa. It is difficult to split and work, but is used for butchers' blocks and for other rough work.
Wood of the species Quercus lobata.

A crystal clear, resinous exudate from the Canadian balsam fir, Abies balsamea, native to northeastern North America. Canada balsam was introduced in Europe in the 18th century. It was used for as varnish, but was found to bloom in moist conditions and darken with age. It is currently used to permanently mount microscopy specimens and as an adhesive for optical lenses. Its softening point is slightly above room temperature.
Class of turpentine used as a solvent in paints, and for medicinal and perfumery purposes; obtained from Canada balsam resin.
Coarse grained, veined marble ranging in color from white to salmon-pink, quarried in and around Candoglia, Piedmont, Italy. It is primarily known as the building material used for Milan Cathedral.

Limestone having a light gray color, occurring widely throughout Great Britain and Ireland, in deposits dating to the Dinantian Epoch of the Carboniferous Period, from about 345 to 280 million years ago.

Sharp, hard, artificial abrasive in solid or powdered form composed of silicon carbide. Available in several degrees of coarseness, it is often used for polishing by abrasion. The finest particles are useful for grinding glass.
Wood from the Pinus caribaea, native to Central America, Cuba, the Bahamas, and the Turks and Caicos Islands.

Refers to a type of marble quarried in the area around Carrara, in Tuscany, Italy. It is characterized by a fine, compact grain and varies in color from pure white to creamy white, sometimes with a bluish tinge; it is a saccharoidal rock that can appear translucent in the finer grades. It has been a favorite stone of sculptors from antiquity to the present, including Michelangelo Buonarotti. Luna marble was the name used in ancient Rome.

A type of structural plate glass that is ground to true plane surfaces; used for storefronts, countertops, tiling, and paneling. It is made in many colors in thicknesses from 11/32 to 1 1/2 inches (0.86 to 3.81 cm) or laminated to give different color effects.
Limestone that is white, with a slight grayish tint, from deposits near Carthage, Missouri.
A variant of Turner's yellow that has a slightly different composition.

A type of hard soap that is mild, odorless, and white or semitransparent, made with olive oil or other vegetable oils and sodium hydroxide or sodium carbonate. Castile soap is used to clean leather (saddle soap) and other items.
Wood of the species Diospyros ebenum. The wood beneath the trunk is pure white until the heart is reached. The heartwood excels in fineness and in the intensity of its dark color.

Wood of the species Albizia odoratissima, having a brown to dark brown heartwood with lighter and darker streaking. It is used for paneling, furniture, carts, wheels, farm implements, and construction timbers. Not a true rosewood.
Wood of the species Chloroxylon swietenia, native to southeast Asia, India, and Sri Lanka. It is a silky, golden-colored wood used for fine cabinetwork, veneer, and farming tools.

Refers to lace made at Chantilly, Oise departement, France, since the 18th century. It is characterized by being delicate, made of silk or linen, and having a six-sided mesh ground and a distinctive floral or scrolled design. Later designs include ribbons, naturalistic flowers, and other designs on a dotted background. Chantilly lace dating from the 19th century was produced in three colors: white, black, and "blond," which was the natural beige color of silk.  It was made into large items such as veils, shawls, other of apparel, and household dresser covers and draperies. Chantilly lace was copied in machine lace from 1840 on.
Fluffiges Garn, das durch Kreuzweben einer Textilie mit gruppenweise angeordneten Kettfäden hergestellt wird, und dann in schmale Streifen zwischen den Gruppen längs  geschnitten wird.

Durchsichtiges, leichtes glattgewebtes Textil aus feinem, eng gezwirnten Garn, ursprünglich aus Seide, heute aus verschiedenen Kunstfasern, er wird beispielsweise für Schals, Kleider, Blusen, Unterwäsche und Schleier verwendet.
Yellow dye produced from the bark of the Chinese buckthorn, historically exported to Europe and elsewhere in small square cakes or crystals. It was also used in medicine.

Soft, thin, unsized, fine-textured, highly absorbent paper of bamboo fiber originating in China, usually of a pale yellow color. It is typically used for steel engraving, woodcut proofs, and as lithographic transfer paper, because it absorbs ink without smearing. Although it is also called "Indian proof paper," it is distinct from "India paper," which is softer and looser in its fiber bond.
A partially decomposed feldspathic granite found in China and used with clay to make oriental porcelain.
Wood of the species Fagus engleriana.

Refers to a fine grade of Prussian blue, having a finer luster and a bronze sheen, favored in printing inks. It is also used in paints, enamels, and lacquers. Considered one of the three best grades of Prussian blue. Chinese blue (BaCuSi4O10) is more stable in chemical structure and fades less easily than Chinese purple.
Wood of Toona sinensis, an Asian mahogany (not really cedar).
Wood of the species Castanea mollissima, native to China. The tree has a strong resistance to the blight that killed off most of the American Chestnut specimens in the 20th century.
Wood of the species Ulmus parvifolia.
Wood of the species Celtis sinensis. It is used for furniture, cooperage, vehicle bodies, and wood ware.
Wood of species Tsuga chinensis, a coniferous Asian tree.

A carbon ink made from burning tung oil with a low oxygen component. Originally in the form of a solid, imported to Europe as compressed sticks. Also, fluid consisting of the pigment suspended in a medium such as water combined with a glutinous binder.

Fairly hard, yellowish-white wax composed primarily of ceryl cerotate and other esters, excreted by the louse-like scale insects of the genus Ceroplastes found in China, Japan, and India. The insects deposit their wax onto the leaves and twigs of Chinese ash trees; it is scraped off, cleaned with hot water, and cast into molds. It is similar to spermaceti and has been used as a substitute for beeswax, for candles, wax paint, polishing leather, and sizing paper.

Chinese purple (also called Han purple) is a synthetic barium copper silicate pigment developed in China at least 2,000 years ago. Chinese purple fades and decomposes over time, particularly in contact with acids or heat.
An unstandardized name for many red pigments, included but not limited to a variety of chrome reds, cinnabar, red mercuric sulfide, and vermilion.
Lightweight silk of plain weave.
Type of dry-process vermilion pigment originally made in China.
A dense zinc oxide pigment first introduced in 1834 as a watercolor paint by Winsor and Newton.
An unstandardized name that has been used for various pigments, including yellow ocher and king's yellow.

Ursprünglich ein gemusterter indischer Stoff, gewöhnlich aus Baumwolle oder Leinen, der in mehreren Phasen des Färbens, Beizens und Reserve-Färbens bemalt und/oder bedruckt wurde. Wurde später mit industriellen Verfahren nachgeahmt, gewöhnlich aus Baumwolle oder Leinen, in mehreren Farben gefärbt. Der Stoff ist in der Regel glänzend.
The resin collected from the Greek island of Chios, having the reputation for the highest quality.
Type of fine-grained, hard, reddish or buff-colored sandstone quarried at Chunar in the Mirzapur District of Uttar Pradesh, and widely used in the architecture of India.
Oolitic limestone, quarried in the vicinity of Clipsham, Leicestershire, England; used primarily as a building stone.

Medieval Mudéjar textile type, a two sided non-reversible silk fabric with a herringbone twill ground with patterned motifs such as lozenge shapes, fleurs-de-lis, or lions. It is a complex silk weave with one warp system and two equal weft systems. Cloth of Aresta is preserved in many European churches as well as Las Huelgas, Burgos.
Bobbin lace with continuous threads decorated with geometric motifs. Inspired by 17th century Genoese bobbin lace, Cluny has become one of the most widely produced hand-made laces.

A type of glass characterized by its mainly greenish, turquoise, yellow, brown-green, or smoky black color accented by air bubbles, streaks of pink and white, and speckles of aventurine. To produce Clutha glassware, a worker used a gather of pale ruby or pale yellow glass, then rolled it over a marver lightly sprinkled with bits of colored glass and mica flakes. The gather was reheated and blown and tooled into the finished product. It was originally made by James Couper & Sons, Glasgow. It is called "Clutha" from Gaelic for "cloudy."

A type of art glass characterized by large air bubbles of various sizes combined with larger unsifted glass particles, and cased with heavier clear glass. Cluthra glassware is made from a lump of crystal glass on a blowing iron, which is rolled across a marver that had been sprinkled with powdered glass of any desired color. It was developed at Steuben Glass Works before 1930 as a variation on the very similar Clutha glass.
Building material, composed of ceramic similar to stoneware, developed in England around 1769, used primarily for architectural decoration.

A fine grained, magnesium-rich white marble from Cockeysville, Maryland; it has a few pale streaks or bands which give an effect of pale gray. One of the most popular types of marble used in nineteenth-century buildings in Washington, D.C., it comprised the top two thirds of the Washington Monument as well as the monolithic columns of the Capitol building extensions.

A fine-grain marble quarried near Marble in Gunnison County, Colorado. Colorado Yule marble is usually pure white but can have pale gray clouds and/or creamy veins. Some regions of the marble belt have a pale blue-gray color. Colorado Yule has been used for statuary and was used for the exterior of the Lincoln Memorial.
Ein cremiges oder flüssiges Präparat, das im Haar oder auf der Haut angewandt wird.

A medium hard variety of copal resin obtained from both living trees and as fossil resin from the genus Copaifera in western Africa. Congo copal is insoluble in most organic solvents until it has been thermally treated. It forms transparent gels with some alcohols, hot oils and and hot solvents. Congo copal is used in varnishes and paints for metal surfaces.

A predominantly pale green marble with white markings that is quarried near Clifden in County Galway, Ireland. A metamorphosed dolomitic limestone, it is considered to be one of two true marbles native to the British Isles, the other being Iona marble.

A weathering steel that has superior corrosion resistance over regular carbon steel because of a protective oxide film on the metal's surface. Its 50,000 minimum yield strength allows for cost reduction because lighter sections can be designed and built into structures; it is generally meant to be used in unpainted applications where a reduction in maintenance costs, such as painting, are desired. Cor-Ten is a brand name for corrosion resistant products that were developed by United States Steel; Cor-Ten has subsequently been licensed to be produced by other steel manufacturers.
Glass decorated with coral-like sprays created with enamel and applied glass beads fused to the object.

A patinated alloy of copper with varying amounts of gold and silver, said to have been developed in Corinth in the second century BCE, and highly valued for ornament and statues. Objects cast from the metal were subjected to a heating and quenching process that resulted in a lustrous purple-black patina resistant to tarnishing.
Light silver-gray granite usually from one of four areas between Bodmin Moor and Land's End in Cornwall, England, the most important quarries being between Falmouth, Camborne, and Helston.
Extremely hard wood of the species Diospyros melanoxylos, native to India, having a black heartwood and a fine, even grain.
Wood made from a Corsican variety of Austrian pine, which is found throughout Europe and western Asia.
A type of Alabama cream marble that is hard, fine-grained, and creamy white in color; similar to Madre Cream.

Term used in the glass trade for an opal glass which has been acid finished and had various ornate designs embedded in the glass in shades of brown and beige. A type of art glass, developed by the Mt. Washington Glass Company.
Wood made from the Pinus cubensis.
A permanent, yellowish-green earth pigment obtained from mineral deposits on Cyprus.
Paper having a distinctive marbling design with swirls and Turkish spots.
Trademarked name for the textile fiber made of polyethylene terephthalate. Originally manufactured and developed by Du Pont.
Steel ornamented with wavy patterns and noted for its hardness; formerly used especially for making sword blades.
Gemusterter gewobener Stoff von damastartigem Aussehen, mit kontrastierendem Glanz in Muster und Grund.

Gewebter gemusterter Stoff mit einer Kette und einem Schuss, bei dem das Muster durch den Kontrast der Bindungsgewebe gebildet wird und auf der Vorder- und der Rückseite in entgegengesetzter Position erscheint.
Kleine flauschige Feder, die am Körper eines erwachsenen Vogels anliegt und in besonderem Maße in seiner feinen Textur ausgebildet ist; ist bei Enten, Gänsen und andere Wasservögeln zu finden.
A type of granite quarried in the vicinity of Dedham, Massachusetts.
Ein schwerer, in Köperbindung gewebter kettbündiger Stoff.
Kalkhaltiges Material, ähnlich dem Knochen, aber härter und dichter, die Hauptteile des Zahn bildend.
Eine Substanz oder Präparat, das Geruch tilgt oder verdeckt.
Baumwolle mit gerippten Streifen oder Karomuster.
An early variety of chromium oxide green.
Refers to any of the various double stranded nucleic acids found in chromosomes of higher organisms which store and transfer genetic information.
A fine-grained, medium hardness, calcitic white marble, sometimes with a slight golden hue was quarried at Iscehisar, ancient Dokimeion/Docimium.

Schwerer Stoff, der aus zwei separaten, gleichzeitig übereinander gewebten Textilien besteht. Die Kette besteht aus zwei Reihen von Enden, die jeweils mit ihrem eigenen Schuss oder mit einem gemeinsamen Schuss verwoben sind, der abwechselnd mit jeder Reihe arbeitet. Bei gemusterten Doppelgeweben wechseln die beliden Textilien ihre Position wie es das Muster erfordert.
Wood from trees of the genus Pseudotsuga.
Grober, fester Leinen- oder Baumwollköper von unterschiedlichem Gewicht und mit einer Kettseite; wird für haltbare Kleidung und Einrichtungsgegenstände verwende.
Beschreibt einen Stoff, der bedruckt oder gestempelt ist.

Belgische Klöppelspitze, die mit feinem Faden in breiten Sträußen angefertigt wird. Sie ist gekennzeichnet durch florale, geklöppelte, manchmal Bandmustern ähnelnden Motiven, die durch eine Fadenführung verbunden sein können. Sie wurde seit etwa 1840 das ganze 19. Jahrhundert hindurch in Brüssel und Brügge hergestellt. Viele Beispiele werden als minderwertig im Vergleich zu anderer in Brüssel hergestellter Spitze angesehen, allerdings war sie preiswert und ein Verkaufserfolg.

Strapazierfähige, dicht gewebte Textilie in Leinwandbindung, früher aus Leinen hergestellt, heute gewöhnlich aus Baumwolle, von unterschiedlichem Gewicht; dem Canvas sehr ähnlich, aber feiner und leichter. Will allgemein für Zelte, Gürtel und Kleidung verwendet.
Kot von Tieren, selten von Menschen, insbesondere bei der Verwendung zur Düngung des Bodens oder, wenn getrocknet, als Baumaterial verwendet.

An easily worked, fine-grained siliceous sandstone from Wisconsin that is buff or cream in color. Like many varieties of sandstone, it is soft when first quarried but gradually hardens as its moisture evaporates.
Distinctive German paper, embossed or printed in gold or silver, with patterns which imitated brocades, damasks, or popular decorative and figurative images of the 18th and early 19th centuries.

A type of symmetric, thick and sturdy bobbin lace with very dense motifs. It was made in the 17th century in Flanders to be sold on the Dutch market. It is sometimes referred to as "Brabant lace" but is not to be mistaken with the lace made in Brussels from the 18th century onwards.
Marbled paper where colors are mingled with a wired comb within the prepared bath, which produces "scallops" of colors, as opposed to "veins".
An alloy of copper and zinc, often used as a substitute for gold leaf since the 19th century.
A compound solution of potassium chlorate and hydrochloric acid used as a mordant in etching.
A transparent yellow lake pigment used since the 16th century. It was initially extracted from unripe buckthorn berries (rhamnetin), and later from oak bark (quercitron).

White lead produced by a historical method of casting metallic lead as thin buckles, which were corroded with acetic acid in the presence of carbon dioxide, using pots of vinegar, dung, and tanner’s bark. After six to fourteen weeks the blue-grey lead was corroded to white lead.
A type of britannia metal that contains 81.5% tin, 7.5% antimony, and 9.5% copper.

Wood from the species Dalbergia latifolia, native from the Himalayas to the southern tip of India and on the island of Java in Indonesia. The wood is fragrant and decorative, used to make premium-quality furniture, panelling, veneers and musical instruments.

Strong chelating agent used to sequester and remove metallic salts. A colorless crystalline compound, EDTA is used in medicine as an anticoagulant; stabilizes bleach in detergents; is used in conservation to clean stains from stone.

A synthetic inorganic colorfast pigment having coarse, irregular particles and ranging in color from a powdery blue to a royal blue. It is composed of a double silicate of calcium and copper, prepared by heating a mixture of silica, copper salts, and calcite in a sodium carbonate flux, forming a stable blue frit. Egyptian blue is stable in all types of media and is unaffected by acids or alkali. It was used by the ancient Egyptians over 5000 years ago as a pottery glaze and watercolor pigment in wall paintings. Similar materials, later manufactured in Italy and called Pompeian blue and Pozzuoli blue, have similar chemical compositions and optical properties.
A coarse breccia marble containing fragments of pophyry, basalt, quartz, and granite in a greenish or purplish ground.
A turquoise colored pigment used in ancient Egyptian and Mediterranean wall painting, Egyptian green is similar in chemical composition to Egyptian blue with which it is sometimes confused.

Material, das von Eiern stammt und sich im Zusammenhang mit Kunstobjekten normalerweise auf die ellipsenförmigen Schalen und den embryonalen Inhalt von Vogeleiern bezieht. Für Gemälde wird der Inhalt als Mittel für Temperafarbe verwendet. Das ganze Ei, Dotter und Eiklar werden manchmal mit Öl und/oder Harz gemischt. Das Eigelb ist eine stabile Emulsion einer wässrigen Lösung mit einem öligen, proteinhaltigen Medium, das schnell zu einer harten, unlöslichen Schicht trocknet. Das Eiklar ist als ein Mittel für Bilderhandschriften verwendet worden. Es wird auch zur Beschichtung mit Blattgold benutzt. Eiweiß (Albumin) ist die klebende Substanz des Eiklars. Als pure Schicht ist Eiweiß klar, spröde und wasserlöslich. Die Wasserlöslichkeit kann durch Erhitzung oder das Zusetzen von Gerbstoff vermindert werden. Für das Äußere eines Vogeleis einschließlich der zerbrechlichen Schale aus Keratin und Kalzit, siehe "Eischale (Tiermaterial)".
Zusätzliche Materialschicht, die zwischen einem Stoff und seinem Futter zum Verstärken, zum Wärmen, zum Formen oder um den Halt des fertigen Produkts zu verbessern, angebracht wird.
Negativ geladenes Elementarteilchen, das konstitutiv für alle Atome ist und sich auf einer Kreisbahn um den Kern befindet. Es wurde 1897 von J. J. Thomson entdeckt.
A fine, black marble from Attica, prized in ancient Greece, especially for friezes and interior decoration.

Eine Form von Dentin, die in den Stoßzähnen ausgewachsener Elefanten gefunden wird, sowie ähnliches Material von Säugetieren mit Stoßzähnen oder großen Zähnen, wie zum Beispiel Walrösser oder Narwale.
Composite pigment made with Bremen blue and a fustic lake; not used for permanent painting.
Ei des Emu-Weibchens. Der Emu ist ein australischer Laufvogel und der zweitgrößte lebende Landvogel. Das Emu-Ei hat eine dunkelgrüne Schale und ist im Durchschnitt etwa 13 cm lang.

Wood of Picea engelmannii, found in western North America. soft, It is a light wood, yellowish to reddish, and fine-grained; used for lumber, millwork, construction, and the manufacture of paper and boxes.
A type of britannia metal that contains 94% tin, 5% antimony, and 1% copper.
General term for wood of cherry trees found in England.
Wood of the species Ulmus procera, found mainly in England and Wales.

A type of mixed lace of which the closed parts are made from Brussels Duchesse lace and the spaces in between are filled with a light mesh in needle lace. Characteristic of English lace are the refined patterns with natural motifs and elegant decorations such as stars, reliefs, and eyelets, applied in both bobbin and needle lace.

Wood of the species Quercus robur, having a coarse but straight grain that is usually a rich brown color. Flat sawn timber has a distinctive figuring while radial cut lumber has silver grain lines. The wood has a high gallic acid content that will corrode iron and other contacting metals. English oak is widely used in furniture, paneling, railway sleepers, cabinet, musical instruments, and ship building.
Pewter of the 16th century containing 91% tin and 9% antimony.
Type of wet-process vermilion, originally made in England.

Wood of the species Juglans regia, native to the Himalayas, Iran, Lebanon, Asia Minor, and Greece. It was introduced into Britain in the mid-15th century. It has a uniform, deep brown color with a medium texture and a close, straight grain. It varies considerably in color the sapwood being pale straw with grayish-brown heartwood with infiltrations of coloring producing a darker-colored streaky appearance.  It is strong but easy to work and polishes to a high gloss. It is used for furniture, veneers, cabinets, paneling, gun stocks. Its burls are especially prized in the furniture trade. For the darker wood from the species Junglans nigra found in the eastern United States, use "black walnut."

Farbloses, leicht entzündliches Kohlenwasserstoffgas, das hauptsächlich aus Methan und anderen einfachen Alkanen besteht und in der Natur in Verbindung mit Erdöl vorkommt und als Brennstoff verwendet wird.

Wood of any of several varieties of the species Fraxinus excelsior. The wood is whitish to pale brown in color with a pink cast. It is a dense wood that is tough and elastic with a straight grain and slightly coarse texture. It is moderately durable but susceptible to insect attack. The timber is used for ladders, tool handles, oars, poles, wheels, boats, weapons, furniture, gymnasium equipment, tennis racquets, and hockey sticks.

Soft, yellowish-white wood of the species Populus tremula, native to Europe and Asia. It is easy to work, but weak, prone to warping, and produces a disagreeable odor when wet. The lightweight, spongy wood is generally used for matches, excelsior, fruit crates, and paper pulp.

Wood of the tree species Fagus sylvatica, native to Eurasia and Great Britain. It is a reddish-brown, close-grained wood that bends well and gives a smooth shiny finish. It is a commonly used timber for flooring, cabinetry, furniture, veneer, tool handles, turnery, and was occasionally used in panel paintings in western Europe.
Wood from the European birch tree, Betula pubescens.

Wood of the species Castanea sativa, native to southwestern Europe, North Africa, and Asia Minor. The heartwood is yellowish-brown in color, closely resembling oak but lacking its silver grain characteristics. It is used in the making of furniture, coffin boards, turnery, and fence poles.
Wood of the Alnus viridis.
Wood of the species Celtis australis. It is used for furniture, cooperage, vehicle bodies, and wood ware.
Wood of the species Ostrya carpinofolia, native to Europe. It is used locally for fence posts and small articles such as tool handles.
Wood of the species Carpinus betulus, native to Europe, Asia, and Iran. It is used for piano actions, turnery, billiard cues, and violin bridges.
Any non-urushi lacquer coating, first seen during the 18th century in Europe, when Oriental lacquerware became very popular and was often imitated.
Wood of Larix decidua, found from Bavaria to the Swiss Alps. Heartwood ranges in color from reddish brown to brick-red in color, and the timber is used for posts, poles, and boat planks.

Wood of the species Quercus cerris, native to Europe and Asia Minor. It is a hard, strong wood, used for furniture, construction, coffins, wagons, boats, tools, roofing, statuary, and veneer. It is beautifully mottled due to the abundance of its silver grain; however, the wood is susceptible to cracking and splitting when drying, which sometimes restricts its use to rough work.
Verdauungsrest, der vom Körper ausgeschieden wird.

Glatt gewebter Stoff, ursprünglich aus Seide, heute auch aus Wolle, Baumwolle und synthetischen Fasern hergestellt, der sich durch leichte flache Rippen im Schuss auszeichnet und zum Beispiel für Damen-Anzüge und Kleider, Hüte, Besatz und Innenausstattungen verwendet wird

Trademark name for a type of glass developed by Tiffany and Company with an iridescent surface simulating that of excavated ancient Roman glass; this effect was produced by spraying the surface of the glass while hot with metallic salts.

Feiner, weicher, dicker, haariger Mantel des Säugetiers, in der Regel aus einem Doppelhaarkleid bestehend; eine Schicht aus vergleichsweise kurzem, weichem, lockigem Haar mit Widerhaken auf der Haut, geschützt durch eine Schicht längerer, glatterer und steiferer Haare, die durch die weichere Haarschicht hindurchwächst.
Chemical oxidant; a solution of hydrogen peroxide and iron sulfate used for in-situ oxidation of organic contaminants such as trichloroethylene.

Bezeichnet eine Spitze, die auf feinem, geknoteten Netz entweder aus Quadrat- oder Diamantmaschen hergestelltt wird; das Netz wird auf einen Rahmen gespannt und bestickt; diese Kunstform besitzt antike Ursprünge. Gewöhnlich wird das Netz hergestellt, indem ein um ein längliches Weberschiffchen gewickelter Faden über einen Gewebestock oder eine Schablone, die die Größe der Maschen bestimmen,  und in die vorher angefertigten Quadrate oder Diamanten geführt wird; ein Knoten, der dem von Fischern ähnelt, wird angezogen und hält den Faden an seinem Ort. Die auf das Netz gesetzte Stickerei kann in einer Vielzahl von Stichen ausgeführt werden, oft sind Stopfstiche und Füllungen darunter. Der Begriff kommt aus dem Französischen, “filet”, und bedeutet “Netzwerk”.

Ein Stoff, der gewöhnlich aus Wolle und Pelzfasern besteht, die oft mit natürlichen oder synthetischen Fasern gemischt werden, in dem die losen Fasern durch die Einwirkung von Hitze, Feuchtigkeit, Chemikalien und Druck miteinander verbunden werden, ohne sie zu spinnen, weben oder stricken.
Freiligender Stein, gefunden auf oder im Erdboden.
Die Schuppe eines Fisches.
Weicher Köper mit leicht aufgerauter Oberfläche, der in verschiedenen Gewichten aus Wolle und weichem Baumwollgarn hergestellt wird, oft in Kombination mit synthetischem Garn.
A further evolution of 16th and 17th century Old-Flemish lace. The motifs are mostly flowers, vines or animals and often encircled with decorative thread.
Hard, yellowish paving brick.

Bezeichnet sehr dünne Plättchen oder Blätter, Pailletten oder Streifen oder Fäden aus Gold oder Silber. Es kann sich auch auf Nachahmungen aus Kupfer, Messing oder einer gold- oder silberfarbenen Legierung beziehen.

Gewebte Textilie, bei der sich eine schlingen- oder büschelförmige Oberfläche über das gesamte Grundgewebe erstreckt. Der Flor wird durch zusätzliche Fäden oder Garne gebildet, die in Richtung der Länge oder der Breite des Gewebes verlaufen.
Plastic laminate made by bonding layers of materials such as canvas, glass, paper, or linen with thermosetting resins. May be used to refer to the trademarked product and to similar materials.
A gray and white mottled marble, sometimes also with red blotches, from Nassau, Germany. It often contains a large quantity of fossils.
Überreste, Eindrücke oder Spuren von Tieren oder Pflanzen aus älteren Erdzeiten, die sich in der Erdkruste erhalten haben.

Artificial black pigment made of the sediment of white wine mentioned in historic documents of the 17th-19th centuries. Frankfurt black was the preferred pigment for making intaglio printing ink. Production of the pigment centered in the vineyard areas east of Frankfurt, Germany in the villages along the banks of the river Main. For unknown reasons, Frankfurt black disappeared after 1900. It is not to be confused with the mixture of bone black and Berlin blue still used as a painting pigment and known as Frankfurt or Parisian black.
Besatz, der aus langen oder kurzen geraden oder gedrehten Fäden, Kordeln oder Quasten besteht; oft in verschiedenen Mustern gruppiert oder geknotet.
Wood from the tree, Abies fraseri, which has in the past been classified as a subspecies of balsam fir.

A high grade of massive talc occurring in the Appalachian region in the United States that can be cut to shape and used for marking purposes; it is usually white in color but may be colored gray or yellow by impurities.
Narrow strips of deep-fried potato, generally eaten as side dish, or as a snack.

A high quality zinc oxide pigment prepared by the French process, which is an indirect technique that vaporizes metallic zinc; the vapors react with air and precipitate as pure zinc oxide. Most commercial zinc oxide pigments are made by this method.
A lake pigment colorant made from archil.
Type of interlocking roof tile, heavily corrugated and formed with interlocking side joints and a rounded bull nose butt edge to engage the corrugations of the course immediately below.
Kettsamt mit ungeschnittenen Schlingen auf einer oder beiden Seiten. Oft aus Baumwolle oder Leinen, wird wegen seiner Saugfähigkeit für Handtücher, Bademäntel und ähnliches genutzt.
Teil einer Pferderüstung, das Brust und Schultern schützt.
Schmales verziertes Band, Spitze oder Borte, oft mit Metallfäden und an beiden Rändern gebördelt, das als Besatz oder Einfassung von Kleidung oder textilen Ausstattungen genutzt wird.
Durchgehender Strang, entweder einfach oder zusammengesetzt, der aus Fadenfasern durch haspeln, spinnen, zwirnen oder mulinieren hergestellt wird.

Gewebter Stoff bei dem die Bindung durch eine Verschiebung der durch den Schuss gesicherten Kettfäden erreicht wird, was gewöhnlich, wenn auch nicht immer, zu einer sehr offenen Bindung führt; wird insbesondere für chirurgische Verbände und als Verstärkung für Papier, insbesondere für Landkarten,  verwendet.

Any of several coarse-grained, pale-colored marbles quarried in northern Georgia. White Georgia is used for statuary. The others, such as Georgia Cherokee and Silver Georgia gray, are used as interior and exterior building stones.
Non-standardized name for various organic black pigments.

Copper-nickel-zinc alloy, typically 20% zinc, 18% nickel, and the balance copper, used as a base metal for plated silverware, for springs and contacts in electrical equipment, and for corrosion-resistant objects.
Dünnes, seidenes Material durch Spinnen und die Larven einiger Insekten gesponnen.

Netzgewebe aus Fäden oder Schnüren, die in regelmäßigen Abständen verknotet oder verflochten sind. Netze wurden ursprünglich manuell gefertigt, werden heute aber üblicherweise maschinell hergestellt. Netze können von spitzenähnlichen Geweben bis zu groben Netzen für Sport oder Jagd reichen. Für Objekte, die aus einer netzartigen Anordnung von Fäden, Zwirn oder Garn bestehen, die gedreht, verknotet oder in regelmäßigen Abständen miteinander verflochten sind, siehe ["Netz (Objekt)"](300230818). Ein Maschengewebe aus Fäden oder Schnüren, das in regelmäßigen Abständen geknotet oder gewebt sind. Netze wurden ursprünglich manuell gefertigt, werden heute aber üblicherweise maschinell hergestellt. Netze können von spitzenähnlichen Geweben bis zu groben Netzen für Sport oder Jagd reichen. Für Objekte, die aus einem Geflecht  von Fäden, Schnüren oder Seide bestehen, welche in regelmäßigen Abständen miteinander verdreht, verknotet oder verwoben wurden, siehe ["Netz (Objekt)"](300230818 ).

Im allgemeinen eine Textilie, die gewebt, gefilzt, gestrickt, gewalkt oder auf andere Weise zu einem flachen Stück verarbeitet wird. Für Textilien in Form von durchgehenden Strängen die aus Faserfäden durch Spulen, Spinnen, Drehen oder Mulinieren hergestellt wird, siehe "Garn".
Besatz, der durch das Um wickeln eines Fadens oder eines Drahtes mit Seide, Kammgarn oder Baumwollfaden entsteht, sodass eine flache durchbrochene Tresse entsteht.

Eine amorphe, anorganische Substanz, die durch Verschmelzen von Kieselsäure (Siliziumdioxid) mit einem basischen Oxid hergestellt wird; im allgemeine transparent, aber oft auch durchscheinend oder undurchsichtig. Ihre charakteristischen Eigenschaften sind ihre Härte und Steifigkeit bei normalen Temperaturen, ihre Fähigkeit zur plastischen Verformung bei höheren Temperaturen und ihre Beständigkeit gegen Witterungseinflüsse und die meisten Chemikalien außer Flusssäure. Sie wird sowohl für Gebrauchs- als auch zu Dekorationszwecken verwendet und kann in verschiedene Formen gebracht, gefärbt oder dekoriert werden. Etwa 3.500 vor Chr. wurde Glas in Mesopotamien als Glasur hergestellt, und die ersten vollständig aus Glas hergestellten Gegenstände stammen aus der Zeit um 2.500 v. Chr.

Limestone formed from pelagic sediment, specifically the ancient ocean bottom layer of soft mud, or “ooze," made from the shells of dead sea creatures belonging to the genus Globigerina. It is the chief building material of the Maltese islands.
Marbled paper prepared in a similar fashion as the spotted technique used in antique marbled paper, but spots are in blue.
Formerly copyrighted name for a series of alkyd resins used for surface coatings such as varnishes, shellacs and paints. Glyptal is made by heating phthalic anhydride and glycerin together.

Als Teil der dritten Stufe der manuellen Blattgoldproduktion: Stapel kleiner Goldquadrate, die von der "Lotform" entfernt und in kleinere Stücke geschnitten wurden, durchschossen mit feiner Goldschlägerhaut; das Ganze wird dann mit Pergamentstreifen gebunden. Die Stapel werden von Hand geschlagen, um Blattgold herzustellen.
Verschiedene Arten von goldfarbenen Tressen, insbesondere auf Uniformen.
Incendiary liquid mixture known to the Byzantine Greeks, used especially to set fire to ships in naval warfare.
A purplish red marble, sometimes with white spots, found in the valley of Barouse and other areas in the French Pyrenees.

Substanz, die auf einigen Küsten und Inseln, die auf ihren Flugrouten von Vögeln frequentiert werden, zu finden ist; es ist im Wesentlichen zersetzter Kot, der reich an Phosphat und stickstoffhaltigen Substanzen ist und als Dünger verwendet wird. Bezieht sich auch auf die Exkremente von Fledermäusen.

Cloth exported from India as a product that could be traded by European merchants in exchange for African goods. Broadly covers a wide range of cheap, brightly colored fabrics, often striped, checkered, or plain.
Kot bestimmter Tiere, insbesondere Haustiere; kann auch für Exkremente verwendet werden, die mit verschiedenen Chemikalien angereichert und als Dünger Verwendung finden.

Die faserförmige Ausstülpung der Haut verschiedener Lebenwesen, bestehend aus dem Protein Keratin; wird mit anderen Fasern zur Herstellung von Geweben, als Füllmaterial und zum Herstellen von Bürsten oder Pinseln genutzt.
A type of britannia metal that contains 87% tin, 7.5% antimony, and 5.5% copper.

Refers to pigment of various varieties of bright yellow color, commonly used as inexpensive substitutes for cadmium yellows in paints, plastics, rubber and coated paper. The term was originally a brand name yellow synthetic organic pigments, but now refers to the entire class of yellow monoazo colorants.
Freestone from a quarry in Haslebury in Wiltshire, England.
Wood from the species Fraxinus floribunda.
Wood from the species Betula utilis.
Süße, zähflüssige Substanz, von Bienen aus dem gesammelten Nektar von Blüten produziert.

A non-continuous lace named for a town in East Devon, where it is thought to have originated from around the 18th century. Inspired by Brussels lace, typical motifs are roses, thistles and cloverleaves.

A medium green pigment that may occur in various formulations. It was formerly composed of a mixture of Prussian blue and gamboge; however, this composition discolored in sunlight when the gamboge faded. A more recent permanent mixture of this name is composed of phthalocyanine green and Hansa yellow or cobalt yellow.

Auswuchs aus veränderter Haut oder verklebtem Haar, der, häufig paarweise, aus dem frontalen Knochen auf jeder Seite des Schädels herausragt und aus einer lebenslang wachsenden Kutikula aus Keratin besteht. Zu unterscheiden von ["Geweih"](300011796), das aus Knochensubstanz besteht.
Utility gypsum cement used in a variety of art applications such as casting.

A bluish white marble with bands of parallel gray lines that originates from Mount Hymettus, near Athens, Greece. It can sometimes be slightly green in color, due to the presence of tiny veins of talc and it gives off a fetid odor when sawn or rubbed which is why it is sometimes called onion marble or marmor cipolla. Hymettian marble was often used for both pagan and Christian sarcophagi.

Transparent, naturally-occurring calcite crystal used in demonstrating the polarization of light.  An oblong stone made of Iceland spar found in the wreck of a 16th-century English warship in 2013 has led archaeologists to speculate that the crystal is one and the same as the sunstone, a near-mythical navigational aid said to have been used by Viking mariners.

In the glass trade, a type of art glass developed and produced by the Württembergische Metallwarenfabrik, Göppingen, ca. 1930, characterized by decorations created through inlaid patches or strips of color, often in combination with patterned air bubbles.
Haar des Iltis, dunkel in der Farbe; häufig aufgrund ihrer Elastizität für Pinsel verwendet.

A nickel-chromium alloy, developed by the International Nickel Co., that is used for food and dairy equipment. It contains 79.5% nickel, 13% chromium, 6.5% iron, and 0.08% carbon, as well as small amounts of copper, manganese, and silicon.

Inks made of carbon black pigment ground in water with water-dispersed resin used as a liquid drawing medium. Distinguished from "black carbon ink," which is made from powdered carbon pigment, usually lampblack, in a dilute aqueous solution of gum or glue.

A very thin, opaque Asian paper that was made in China and imported to Europe by the Dutch East India Company from about 1768 to 1875.  India paper is thin with a basis weight of 20 pounds with approximately 1000 pages to an inch, used for printing thin paper editions such as bibles and reference books. In the late 19th century, it was replaced for this use by an imitation called "Bible paper," which is a rag paper originally manufactured by Oxford University Press.
Wood from the species Catalpa bignonioides, lightweight with a fine grain. It is used for ornamental items and cabinetry.
Wood of the species Aesculus indica, common in the Himalayan lowlands.

A deep red lake color made from lac dye precipitated with precipitated with alumina trihydrate. Indian lake was once an important colorant used since ancient times. It has since been replaced by synthetic colors.
An unstandardized name for various red dye colorants.

Synthetically produced brick red color iron oxide artists' pigment. Originally, the name "Indian red" was used for red earth pigments imported from the Persian Gulf and India; in the early 18th century, a synthetic Indian red composed of pure ferric oxide was made from steel mill wastes. It is a dense, opaque, permanent pigment.

A yellow organic pigment composed of magnesium euxanthate, originally made from the urine of cows fed exclusively on mango leaves, producing a washed, powdered material that is a clear, golden yellow color. Used for  watercolor and tempera paints. The production of Indian yellow was prohibited in 1908 because the mango leaves were harmful to the cattle. Current counterparts are made with a synthetic coal-tar derivative. Authentic Indian yellow fluoresces a bright yellow color when lit with long wavelength ultraviolet light.

A noncrystalline massive rock with aggregate filler and a matrix of 98% calcium carbonate in gray and buff colors; it is an oolitic limestone with small round grains resembling fish roe. Almost half of all the limestone used in the United States in block form is Indiana limestone.

A rare, naturally occurring metallic element with symbol In and atomic number 49. Indium is a soft, silver-white metal collected as a residue of zinc-processing operations and chiefly used in the manufacture of semiconductors. Also appears as an impurity in ancient metal objects.

International Klein Blue (IKB) is a powdery ultramarine pigment created by Yves Klein in 1956 and patented by the artist in 1960. He used the color, symbolic of purity of spirit, exclusively from 1957 on.
A low-expansion iron alloy containing about 36% nickel; used for precision surveying instruments and timepieces.
Ein Atom oder eine Gruppe von Atomen, das eine oder mehrere positive oder negative elektrische Ladungen trägt.

A predominantly pale green marble with white markings that is quarried on the island of Iona. A metamorphosed dolomitic limestone, it is considered to be one of two true marbles native to the British Isles, the other being Connemara marble.
A compact marble found in counties Galway and Kilkenny in Ireland that is considered one of the best black marbles.
A large-grained, buff marble quarried in Istria on the Adriatic Sea and on some of the Dalmatian islands.

Marbled paper prepared one of three ways: "blue", which shows a network of blue with small to medium white spots, "four veins", which contains red, yellow, green and blue, and "hair vein marble", which contains a ground color (black), which is driven into tiny veins using water or another substance.
Jeder gewebte, strukturierte Stoff, der auf einem Jacquard-Webstuhl hergestellt wird.

Pale yellow, soft, waxy substance obtained from the berries of Toxicodendron vernicifluum, the sumac tree native to Japan, and Toxicodendron succedaneum. It is not a true wax, but a fat. Japan wax is a byproduct of lacquer manufacture; it contains palmitic acid, stearic acid, and oleic acid with japanic acid. Japan wax is sold in flat squares or disks and has a rancid odor. It is used in the manufacture of candles, pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, floor waxes, furniture polishes, pastels, crayons, buffing compounds, metal lubricants, adhesives, and as a substitute for beeswax.
Wood of the species Fagus japaonica, native to Japan.
Wood of the Cryptomeria japonica tree, an evergreen native to eastern Asia. It is reddish brown, soft, fragrant, and used for construction, furniture, and ornamental carving.
Wood from the chestnut tree native to Japan and other areas of Asia, Castanea crenata.

Wood of the Chamaecyparis obtusa tree, an evergreen native to Japan and East Asia. Wood is reddish-brown, fragrant, durable and fine grain. It is used for Shinto and Buddhist temples and other construction, furniture, and sculpture.

Wood of Larix kaempferi, native to Japan, but planted in Europe and the United Kingdom. The reddish brown timber in similar to that of the European larch, and it also used in the construction of poles and boat planks.

Wood of the species Acer palmatum, native to Japan. It has a creamy white sapwood that is not sharply defined from the heartwood, but it can however, have a reddish tinge. It is used in the manufacture of furniture, paneling, flooring for bowling alleys, dance halls, and warehouses, and for decorative veneer.

Refers to various types of handmade paper, particularly strong, handmade paper composed of long bast fibers derived from the paper mulberry (Broussonetia papyrifera), the gampi (Wikstroemia canascens) and the mitsumata (Edgeworthia papyrifera). Thin, translucent sheets of Japanese paper are often used as a reinforcing paper and hinges because of their strength, flexibility, and permanence. Specifically for paperlike material made from the pith of the rice paper tree, use "rice paper."
Wood from the Pinus pumila, native to northeastern Asia, including Japan.
Oil derived from the nuts of the tree Aleurites cordata. It does not gelatinize when heated and is used for varnishes, waterproofing, paper and soaps.
A thick paper, historically hand-made in Japan, with a smooth finish produced as an imitation of vellum parchment.
Wood of the Pinus parviflora, native to Japan.
General term referring to wood of several species of willow found in Japan, including Salix gilgiana, Salix cardiophylla, and Salix bakko, among others.
Wood from the Pinus jeffreyi, native to the area from southwest Oregon to northern Baja California in Mexico.
Wood from Pinus virginiana, a dwarf pine of North America.

Hard, shiny, tear-shaped seeds of a tropical grass; the seeds are sometimes used for jewelry or rosaries. The seed kernel is edible, and varieties are used as food product in parts of East Asia and in the Philippines.

An aromatic oleoresin obtained from the red pine, Pinus resinosa,  from trees in the Vosges mountains in France. An aromatic oleoresin obtained from the red pine, Pinus resinosa,  from trees in the Vosges mountains in France.
Starkes, dickes Seil mit mehreren Strängen aus Eisendraht. Unterschiedet sich von "Seil", das normalerweise aus synthischen oder organischen Fasern besteht.
Feines schwarzes Pulver, das aus Antimon besteht und als Kosmetikum zum Schwärzen von Augenlidern und Augenbrauen verwendet wird.

Baumwollgewebe, schwerer als Mousselin, einfach, gefärbt oder mit ein- oder mehrfarbigen Mustern bedruckt. Im 18. un 19. Jahrhundert bezog sich der Begriff auf bedruckte, gefärbte oder einfache Gewebe aus Indien; heute bezieht er sich allgemein auf Baumwolldrucke mit kleinen stilisierten Mustern.
Feine Körperbehaarung oder Kamelwolle, wird als Textilfaser verwendet. Für Künstlerpinsel, die aus dem Haar von Eichhörnchen gemacht wurden, verwenden Sie "Kamelhaarpinsel".
Glattes, kompaktes Garn mit durchschnittlicher bis starker Drehung aus langen Wollfasern, die kardiert und gekämmt wurden.
A type of dolomitic limestone found in southern Minnesota.
Positiv geladenes Ion; wird so genannt, weil es bei einer Elektrolyse von der Kathode oder der negativ geladenen Elektrode angezogen wird.

A hard, white, high-strength, quick-setting finishing plaster that takes a high polish; it is made by heating gypsum at a high temperature, grinding it to a powder, and then adding alum to accelerate the set. It is often used as an interior plaster in areas that are treated roughly and in areas with a high level of moisture.
Oolitic limestone quarried in the vicinity of Ketton, Leicestershire, England; used primarily as a building stone.
Florgewebe, bei dem der Flor durch eine zusätzliche Polkette gebildet wird.
Trademark for a type of commercially produced aramid, a synthetic textile material which is lightweight, nonflammable, and has a high impact resistance.

Zusammen mit der “Nadelspitze” eine der wichtigsten Arten von handgemachter Spitze. Charakteristisch sind ihre ineinander verflochtenen Fäden, die auf Rollen oder Spulen gewickelt sind und auf einem Kissen angefertigt werden, auf dem das Muster mit Nadeln abgesteckt ist; die Spitze wird mit beiden Händen angefertigt, indem die Spulen von einer Seite zur anderen bewegt werden und so ein Geflecht oder ein Gewebe (genannt “toile”) bilden. Es werden Blei- oder Elfenbeingewichte verwendet. Wahrscheinlich wurde sie im 16. Jahrhundert in Flandern entwickelt. Frühe Klöppelspitze wurde oft verwendet für Halskrausen und Kragen und zeichnet sich durch stark angeschrägte Zacken an einem schmalen Band aus.
Pewter containing bismuth, used in making utensils.

Rosette, Bänderknoten oder ähnliches Ornament, das gewöhnlich auf einem Hut als Abzeichen eines Amtes oder einer Partei, als Teil einer Dienstbotenuniform oder als Schmuck getragen wurde. Rosette, Bänderknoten oder ähnliches Ornament, das gewöhnlich auf einem Hut als Abzeichen eines Amtes oder einer Partei, als Teil einer Dienstbotenuniform oder als Schmuck getragen wurde.

Außerirdisches Objekt aus gefrorenem Materialien und Felsbrocken, das die Sonne in hohem, elliptischem Orbit umkreist. Wenn es sich der Sonne nähert, entwickelt es in der Regel eine diffuse Nebelhülle aus Gas und einen leuchtenden Schweif.

Ein gewebter Stoff, der durch parallele diagonale Grate oder Rippen gekennzeichnet ist, die dadurch entstehen, dass die Schussfäden über einen und unter zwei oder mehr Fäden der Kette geführt werden, anstatt in regelmäßiger Folge darüber und darunter wie bei der Leinwandbindung. Normaler Köper zeichnet sich durch eine sich regelmäßig wiederholende diagonale Linie aus, die in der Regel in einem Winkel von 45 Grad von links nach rechts und nach oben verläuft. Die Bindung kann auf verschiedene Weisen variiert werden einschließlich der Änderung des Winkels oder der Richtung der Köperlinie, wie beim Fischgratköper.

Bezieht sich auf einen dicken, locker gesponnenen Seidenfaden oder eine Seidenschnur.  Er ist oft aus minderwertiger Seide gefertigt und wird für die Umrandung von Mustern bei der Spitzenherstellung, zur Herstellung von Besätzen und Fransen und auch für andere Anwendungen verwendet, bei denen keine Festigkeit erforderlich ist.

Refers to papers made by traditional papermaking traditions of Korea; may be made from a variety of materials, chiefly mulberry bark, but also hemp, bamboo leaves, rice-straw and pine-needles. Korean paper is typically denser than most Chinese paper and is known for its high purity and durability.
Puder, Lotion, Lippenstift, Rouge oder andere Präparate für den menschlichen Körper für die Verschönerung, Bewahrung oder Veränderung des Aussehens einer Person.
Exkremente, die durch den Anus ausgeschieden werden.
Synthetisches Garn, das sich unter Spannung dehnt und ohne Spannung zusammenzieht; es wird gewöhnlich aus thermoplastischen Faserbestandteilen hergestellt und insbesondere für Strumpfwaren verwendet.

Ein Getränk, das aus einem Aufguß von heißem oder kaltem Wasser und den Blättern oder Blattteilen eines oder mehrerer getrockneter oder frischer Kräuter besteht, manchmal unter Hinzufügung von gemahlenen Gewürzen. Es wird oft aus medizinischen Gründen oder als Erfrischung getrunken.

Leichtes Gewebe aus verschiedenen Faserarten mit einer gekräuselten Oberfläche, die durch die Verwendung von stark gedrehten Fäden oder Garnen, durch Bedrucken mit Natronlauge, durch Weben mit unterschiedlichen Spannungen oder durch Prägung entsteht.
A red powder derived from turmeric, used for making the mark on the forehead in the Hindu religion.

Sedimentary deposits which contain large amounts of preserved fossils. Paleontologists divide them into two subtypes: Konservat-Lagerstätten and Konzentrat-Lagerstätten. The latter relates to desposits of fossils that are concentrated by sedimentological processes which do not preserve details (such as organs), and the former refers to deposits containing a large concentration of fossils that are exceptionally well-preserved.
Gewebte Textilie, in der Regel mit Brokat, in die flache Metallfäden, häufig golden oder silbern, eingearbeitet sind.

Gewebtes textiles Material, das sich durch eine gewebte Struktur auszeichnet bei der eine sekundäre oder bindende Kette die Schüsse, die das Muster bilden, anbindet, wodurch ein Doppelgewebe-Effekt entsteht.
A compact marble of brilliant red color blotched with white and found in Montagne Noir and other regions in the Pyrenees. Resembles griotte marble.
White marble with grey/black veins or speckling quarried in Val Venosta, Alto Adige, Italy.
Wood of Cedrus libani, having a light brown heartwood and a pungent cedar odor and fairly knotty grain. It is typically used for joinery, garden furniture, gates, fences, and doors.

A type of white marble with a medium fine texture from Lee, Massachusetts. It can sometimes be light gray in appearance. A small band of Lee marble was used in the Washington Monument in Washington, D.C. but it proved too costly; it was, however, used to build parts of the Capitol in the 1850s.
Allgemeine Bezeichnung für ein Textil, das aus der gesponnenen Faser des Flachses gewebt wird.
An early variety of cobalt blue.

A serpentinite from the Italian Riviera, between Spezia and Genoa. It is found in two varieties: one is purplish or mottled black and dark red with some green pebbles or veins, the other is green with many white veins. It is difficult to work with but acquires a high polish. It may resemble some varieties of variegated marble from Tennessee in the United States.

A naturally occurring yellow or greenish glass made of silica found in parts of the Libyan Desert, the Western Desert of west Egypt, and along the Libyan-Egyptian border. Its origins remain a debated issue within the scientific community, but may be the result of a meteor impact fusing desert sand. The glass was used as a gemstone by the ancient Egyptians, and also as a material for paleolithic tools.

Embriodered lace from the Belgian town of Lier, made with cotton A form of embroidered net lace produced in the northern Belgian town of Lier, dating from the 19th century and later. The tambour hook is used to make the outlines as well as the different fillings on tulle, with the aim of creating a strong contrast between the open and worked areas of the lace.

Refers to a distinct type of bobbin lace created in Lille, which is now in France but was formerly in Flanders. It was created primarily during the 16th through 19th centuries. It also refers to imitations of Lille lace made later and/or in other locations. It is characterized by a very fine net background formed of a hexagonal mesh in which the thread is twisted rather than plaited. The designs typically include curving flowers and scrolls, and small, square dots scattered over open areas of mesh.

Refers to a distinctive type of textile work made at Mount Kennet, near Limerick, Ireland, from 1829 onwards. It may also refer to similar textiles made in other locations. It comprises machine-made net on which embroidery is applied, with a result that is similar in appearance to true hand-made lace.  It is characterized by having designs that imitate contemporary popular designs; designs are embroidered using tambour stitches for outlines and needlerun stitches for filling. The term is sometimes used loosely to refer to embroidered net in general.

Durchsichtiger, leichter, glatt gewebter Stoff, ursprünglich aus Leinen, heute auch aus fein gekämmtem Baumwollgarn, mit Stärke oder Schlichte ausgerüstet; wird oft für Taschentücher, Schürzen und Vorhänge verwendet.
Kosmetikum, das zur Färbung der Lippen verwendet wird.
A compact yellow marble from Estremoz, Portugal; it somewhat resembles Siena marble but is inferior.
Ein dicker, schwerer und wasserdichter Wollstoff, der insbesondere für Überbekleidung verwendet wird.

Wood of the species Platanus acerifolia. It is a hard, tough, light reddish-brown wood with a fine texture that polishes well, but can be difficult to work. It is commonly used for interiors, construction, tool handles, furniture, cabinetry, flooring, and veneers. Sold commercially as "lacewood" because the quarter-sawed wood has a grain pattern with characteristic flake-shaped markings, with reddish-brown rays contrasting with the lighter colored background resembling lace.
A fine-grained, even-textured sandstone that is reddish-brown in color, quarried near East Longmeadow, Massachusetts. Used extensively for buildings in New England, United States.

Stapel Goldblätter, der von der "Quetsche (Metallbearbeitungswerkzeug)" entfernt und in Quadrate geschnitten wird, in einen Stapel geschichtet, der mit Goldschlägerhaut durchschossen und mit Pergamentstreifen gebunden ist, um in der zweiten Phase der manuellen Blattgoldherstellung dünner geschlagen zu werden, bevor er in die "Goldschlägerform" geschoben wird.

Bezeichnet die Imitation von handgewirkter Spitze, die durch Baumwollstickereimuster auf Seidengrund entstehen; die Seide wird am Schluss durch Chlor oder Natronlauge weggeätzt. Auf diese Art hergestellte Spitzen-Imitationen erschienen erstmals im späten 19. Jahrhundert. Luftspitze ähnelt oft der Venezianischen Spitze, aber sie unterscheidet sich durch das Fehlen des Knopflochstiches, der bei handgefertigten Spitzen vorhanden ist.
Synthetic yarn with reflective properties made of aluminum filaments coated with plastic. It is used in Nigeria and other parts of West Africa to weave shiny, glittering cloth-like material.
Textilie, die aus Baumwolle, Seide oder Leinen als Kette und Wolle, Mir, Alpaca oder einer anderen glänzenden Haarfaser als Schuss hergestellt wird, um eine glänzende Oberfläche zu erzeugen.
Tradename of a coarsely mottled marble from Malletts Bay on Lake Champlain.
Hard, quick-setting cement made of dehydrated gypsum to which is added a small percentage of calcined sodium sulfate and potassium sulfate; used for walls, and for floors, when mixed with sand.

A rubberized cloth made by coating two fabric layers with rubber dissolved in naphtha and then pressing them together. This double cloth was the first coated cloth and was developed by Charles Macintosh in Scotland in 1823 for rainwear use.

Bezeichnet grobe Spitze oder Saum, der hergestellt wird durch Verknoten von Schnüren oder dicken Fäden in geometrischen Mustern. Der Begriff kommt aus dem Türkischen “makrama”, was “Serviette” oder “Handtuch” bedeutet. Sie war eine Spezialität in Genua, wo Handtüchr mit geknoteten Schnüren im 19. Jahrhundert populär waren. Der Vorgänger des Macrame war eine Technik der geknoteten Spitze, bekannt als “punto a groppo”, aus dem 16. Jahrhundert. In den 1960er Jahren wurde Macrame eine berühmte Fertigkeit und kreative Kunsttechnik in Amerika und Europa. Sie wurde für die Fertigung von Lampenschirmen, Pflanzenampeln, Hängematten, Gardinen und Wandbehängen verwendet.
A type of Alabama cream marble that is hard, fine-grained, and creamy white in color; similar to Cream Blanc.

The first commercially available acrylic paint. It was prepared from pigments suspended in a solution of n-butyl methacrylate resin and solvent, developed by Leonard Bocour and Sam Golden in the mid-1940s. Magna could be diluted with turpentine or mineral spirits, had a fast drying time. and could be combined with oil paints. It dried to a hard, matte film. Manufacture of this paint ended in the 1970s.
A soft variety of copal resin obtained from species Agathis dammara native to the Philippines and Indonesia. It is used for varnishes and resin/oil media, and in many fixative recipes.
Malleable resin with a pungent odor obtained from the Pili tree, Canarium indicum of Indo-Malaysia. Malleable resin with a pungent odor obtained from the Pili tree, Canarium indicum of Indo-Malaysia.

Strong fiber obtained from leaf stems of the species Musa textilis, native to the Philippines. Not related to true hemp. It was exported to Europe and elsewhere in the 19th century and is now cultivated in Central America. Fibers are lustrous and range in color from cream to light purple, brown, or black, varying in texture from silky to stiff. It is used for rope, especially in marine environments since it is buoyant and does not swell in water. Coarser fibers are used for heavy cordage, twine, hats, hammocks, manila paper, and tissue paper. Fine, glossy fibers are used for carpets, tablecloths, placemats, purses, rugs, and clothing. Waste fibers are used for papers with high wet strength, such as tea bags.
A compact, semi-crystalline magnesian limestone from Minnesota, ranging in color from buff, cream, yellow, gray, pink, or reddish buff. It is commonly used for building as well as for decoration.

Refers to a brass alloy of copper, zinc, and tin, with the appearance of silver or gold. Invented and largely produced in Mannheim, Germany, it was especially in use in the early 18th century.  Mannheimer gold was often used to make inexpensive jewelry.

A viscous, oleoresin obtained from South American deciduous trees of the genus Copaifera native to Venezuela, containing 40% copaiba oil. A viscous, oleoresin obtained from South American deciduous trees of the genus Copaifera native to Venezuela, containing 40% copaiba oil.

Wood of the species Casearia praecox, native to the West Indies, Venezuela, and Colombia. The wood ranges in color from lemon-yellow to almost white, with little or no distinction between sapwood and heartwood. It is used in the making of shuttles, spindles, piano keys, and mathematical instruments. It is often died black to resemble ebony.
A megilp or gel medium for oil painting, introduced in the United States in the 1940s by Jacques Maroger, a painter and conservator.

Synthetically prepared black iron oxide pigment that is permanent, having a warm brownish black color, existing as a dense, heavy powder, and unlike most naturally produced black iron oxide pigments, it is magnetic. Mars black was developed in the early 20th century; it is used in oil paints, watercolors, and mortars and cements, because it does not effloresce.
Synthetically prepared dark brown iron oxide pigment. Mars brown is a permanent pigment with good tinting strength and good oil-drying properties.

"Mars" is now the common name used for synthetic iron oxide pigments. Mars colors are made from hydrated iron oxide co-precipitated with alum using lime or potash. The proportion of the mixture controls the degree of color; drying the product produces Mars yellow, other colors include orange, red, black, brown, and violet, produced by calcining the yellow. "Mars" was originally a brand name in the late 18th century.
Synthetically prepared red iron oxide pigment. It is a permanent pigment, having good tinting strength and good oil-drying properties, and ranging in color from scarlet to maroon.
Synthetically prepared violet color iron oxide pigment, which is permanent, having good tinting strength and good oil-drying properties.

Synthetically prepared yellow iron oxide pigment having a medium to deep yellow color with good tinting strength. It is permanent, inexpensive, has good-film forming properties, acts as a drier for oil paints, and used extensively in the paint industry to produce cream tints with titanium dioxide.

Traditional type of soap made from olive oil or other vegetable oils, in the area of Marseilles, France since ca. 1370. In 1688 Louis XIV introduced regulations limiting the use of the name "savon de Marseille" to soaps made in and around the Marseilles area and only made from olive oil. Today this law still applies, although other vegetable oils may now be used. Often used for cleaning and degumming textiles.
Cement made with potassium carbonate instead of alum.

Maschinell hergestellte Imitation handgefertigter Spitze. Der Begriff schließt auch Spitze mit Handstickerei auf einem maschinell gefertigten Netzgrund ein, der in England ab den 1770er Jahren hergestellt wurde. Frühes maschinell gefertigtes Gewebe besaß oft verschlungene Stiche, die dazu neigten, auszulaufen; maschinell gefertigtes Gewebe nach 1808 kann durch die Imitation der einfachen Schlingen der Lille-Spitze charakterisiert sein. Maschinell gefertigte Gewebe können maschinell mit Kettenstichen oder Plattstichmuster verziert werden . Maschinell gefertigte Spitze von 1840 oder später kann Klöppelspitze imitieren. Maschinell gefertigte Spitze ist generell charakterisiert durch das Fehlen der klaren Webstruktur der Klöppelspitze.
Ein doppelt gewebter Stoff in gesteppter Ausführung bei dem ein wattierter Schuss zur Verstärkung des Reliefs verwendet wird.
Teil oder Komponente eines Materials.
Greenish blue pigment found on wall paintings and painted objects from several Maya sites, determined in 1967 to be composed of indigo absorbed on a clay substrate.
Bobbin lace characterized by a fine twisted-and-plaited ground with solid (usually floral) designs outlined with a flat gimp. It was originally produced at Mechelen, now in Belgium.
Der innere Kern oder das Mittelteil einer Struktur, wie beispielsweise einees Haares.
Garn, das durch das Verdrehen von zwei oder mehr Strängen verzwirntes Garn; die letzte Drehung ist entgegengesetzt zu der der gezwirnten Enden.

Jedes Haar des Menschen (Homo sapiens), insbesondere das Haar der Kopfhaut. Menschliches Haar ist ein zylindrisches Rohr, das hauptsächlich aus Keratin besteht. Der Haarschaft ist mit dünnen, flachen Schuppen bedeckt und der zentrale Kern enthält oft rote, schwarze oder braune Pigmente. Gewerblich wurde Menschenhaar oft zur Herstellung von Perücken und Pinseln verwendet.
Der Körper oder die Körper einer verstorbenen Person oder eines Volkes im Ganzen oder in Teilen, unabhängig vom Erhaltungszustand.

Interplanetarisches Objekt von relativ geringer Größe, das in die Erdatmosphäre mit hoher Geschwindigkeit betritt, sich erhitzt und die Luft um sich aufheizt, was in einer Leuchterscheinung resultiert, die "Sternschnuppe" genannt wird. Wenn ein solches Objekt den Eintritt in die Atmosphäre überlebt und auf dem Boden einschlägt, wird es als "Meteorit" bezeichnet.
Interrplanetares Teilchen oder Brocken aus felsiger oder metallische Materie, die den Eintritt in die Erdatmosphäre überleben und auf der Erde oder einem anderen Planeten einschlagen.
Lang gewebter Stoff, der per Meter verkauft wird. Die englische Bezeichnung bezieht sich auf die Maßeinheit "Yard".

Plastic laminate made by bonding layers of cloth or paper with a resin under heat and pressure; made in sheet, rod, and tube form, and in other molded shapes. May be used to refer to the trademarked product and to similar materials.

A type of handmade drawing paper that was textured and of high quality. It was a laid paper with a surface having rows of narrow parallel ridges on one side and a rougher, bumpy surface on the other side. A paper of this name is still produced by the Ingres paper mill, but scholars debate whether this paper is the same paper that was so prized by Seurat and other 19th-century artists.
Mixed lace from Milan with a ground in needle lace on which motifs made from mechanically made tape are applied; these motifs most often depict flowers.
Weiße oder gelblich weiße Flüssigkeit, abgesondert von den Milchdrüsen weiblicher Säugetiere.

A coarse biotite granite, quarried in Milford, Massachusetts, composed mostly of light pink feldspar with additions of gray quartz and dark, greenish-black flecks of a chloritic black mica. It is very strong, takes a high polish, and has a fine and close texture, making it one of the most desirable granites quarried in the U.S. for general building as well as decorative purposes.
A high quality grade of ferric ferrocyanide that is usually blended with gypsum or barium sulfate.
A flesh red granite that is found in abundance in Minnesota.
Wood of the species Celtis laevigata. It is used for furniture, cooperage, vehicle bodies, and wood ware.
A white to light gray marble with a blue or gray tint; it is coarse-grained and contains some stylolites.
A variety of emerald green (a toxic copper-based pigment), perhaps invented by Baron von Mitis in Vienna.

Der kleinste Bestandteil einer chemischen Verbindung, der unabhängig existieren kann und noch die Merkmale dieser Verbindung bewahrt. Die Atome, aus denen ein Molekül besteht, sind entweder kovalent gebunden, wie bei Kohlendioxid, oder elektrovalent, wie bei Natriumchlorid.

Mondragone marble is a slightly metamorphosed yellow or gray carbonate rock, which was widely employed as monumental stone in the 18th-century royal palaces of Naples and Caserta. Named for Mondragone, Campania where the marble is quarried.
Alloy composed of about 67% nickel, 28% copper, and 5% other elements such as iron.
Granite quarried near Monson, Massachusetts.

Ein festes Gewebe, in der Regel aus Seide, das in verschiedenen Maschenweiten gewebt wird und zu verschiedenen Zwecken verwendet werden kann, zum Beispiel zum Beuteln von Mehl oder zur Verwendung beim Siebdruck oder bei Handarbeiten.
Brass containing 60% copper and 40% zinc, commonly produced in sheets.

Distinctive glass produced on the island of Murano, located off Venice, Italy. Murano was well-known for glassmaking by the 10th century. Even though Murano glassmakers have emigrated elsewhere, experts and commercial markets usually hold that authentic Murano glass is fabricated only in Murano. Most Murano glass is made using the lampworking technique.

In der Antike verwendete Bezeichnung für eine Faser (und erweitert auch für ein Gewebe). Sie wurde wegen ihrer Feinheit sehr geschätzt und bildete einst die Basis für eine kleine Textilindustrie rund um den italienischen Mittelmeerraum. „Byssos“ wurde genutzt um eine Vielzahl von Materialien zu beschreiben, was zur Verwirrung und zu Kontroversen über die richtige Bezeichnung führte. Einige Wissenschaftler betrachten Byssos als Oberbegriff für eine Vielzahl von Faserproteinen und den daraus hergestellten Produkten; andere bestehen darauf, dass der Begriff sich speziell auf aus Muscheln gewonnene „Seeseide“ beschreibt. Die Verwendung erstreckt sich auf beides.

Sehr feine weiße Baumwolle in Leinwandbindung, die in einer Vielzahl von Qualitäten hergestellt wird; ursprünglich aus Indien, wurde er in Europa und Amerika in großem Umfang für Kleidung hergestellt und manchmal auch von Künstlern genutzt.

A type of art glass developed and produced by the Württembergische Metallwarenfabrik (W.M.F.) by Karl Weidman ca. 1926. It is characterized by the use of colorless, raw glass made iridescent by a silver stain and then matte fired, resulting in a shimmering metallic surface.

Mit der “Klöppelspitze” die am meisten vorkommende von Hand hergestellte Spitze. Sie ist charakterisiert durch ihre Herstellung mit Nadel und Faden auf Fäden, die über ein Muster gelegt werden, wobei grundsätzlich nur eine Stichart verwendet wird: der Knopflochstich und der verknotete Knopflochstich. Es wird vermutet, dass sie in Italien im 15. Jahrhundert entwickelt wurde und von der Durchbruch- und der Ausschnittstickerei auf Leinen beeinflusst ist. Meistens wird das Muster auf ein Stück Papier oder Pergament, das von hinten mit Stoff oder einem anderen Material verstärkt ist, gezeichnet; das Muster wird nachgenäht mit Stichen, die als festigender Rahmen des Stücks dienen. Die Füllung und das Muster werden mit einer Nadel und einem einzigen Faden in einer Folge von Knopflochstichen genäht, wobei der Rücken nicht durchstochen wird; die Stiche werden in dichten Reihen ausgeführt und formen so die festen Teile des Musters und schließlich Maschen. Gerade Linienstiche können hinzugefügt werden, um weitere Stiche zu befestigen; geklöppeltes oder gewebtes Band wird manchmal hinzugefügt. Mit einem Messer werden der Rücken und das fertige Stück voneinander getrennt.

Substanz für eine Brandwaffe, die 1943 für militärische Zwecke entwickelt wurde,  bestehend aus Benzin und durch eine Mischung von Naphthensäuren und Palmitinsäuren verdickt, um ein Gel zu bilden, insbesondere in Flammenwerfer und Brandbomben verwendet.

Warm yellow pigment containing lead antimonate that has been used as a colorant for glass, ceramic tiles, and paint for at least 3500 years. It is probably named after the its resemblance to the yellow minerals found at Mount Vesuvius near Naples, Italy, although it not well documented that the pigment was first made in Naples. The specific color of the pigment depends on the method and temperature of manufacture. It came into widespread use in the 18th century, replacing lead-tin yellow as the pigment most often used by artists in Europe. The term is also used loosely to refer to other pigments imitating Naples yellow but produced by mixing together pigments such as cadmium yellow, zinc white, ochre, and Venetian red.
Faser, die aus einer tierischen, pflanzlichen oder mineralischen Quelle für die Umwandlung in nicht gewebte Textilien (Filz, Papier) oder gewebte Stoffe gewonnen wird.
Brand of strong vinyl-coated cloth made to look like leather.
An ancient, coarse-grain, white marble quarried from the Greek island of Naxos. Naxian marble was used for sculpture between the 7th and 6th centuries BCE.

Volcanic rock produced by the largest known trachytic phreatoplinian eruption and composed of zeolites such as phillipsite and chabazite. Neapolitan yellow tuff represents the most used building stone of the historical monumental heritage of Naples and also constitutes a significant part of the city modern architecture.
A particular blue pigment that was distributed in drop form.
Wood of the species Nothofagus solandri, native to New Zealand.  It is brown in color and known for its high strength and durability.
Wollstoff mit einem Samtflor.

Vegetable-tanned goatskin from Nigeria. Similar to "oasis" but tanned locally and dyed in fewer colors, the most common being brick-red, green, and buff. It is highly prized by bookbinders for its high quality and grain pattern.
A copper alloy containing copper (89%), aluminum (5%), zinc (5%), and tin (1%).
Brick whose nominal dimensions are 2 2/3 by 4 by 12 inches.

Refers to a type of glass that was invented during the Gothic Revival in the 19th century and is characterized by an uneven thickness that is thicker in the center, and thinner on the edges. It is made by blowing the molten mixture into a rectangular mold, or by blowing and molding a bubble of glass into a box shape, then cutting the sides of the box to form rectangles of glass. The glass may be clear or colored.

A dark bluish gray sandstone quarried on the west side of the Catskill Mountains near the Delaware River (formerly North River) in New York. In the 19th century, North River bluestone was used for sidewalks and curbs.
Wood from the Pinus resinosa, found in North America.
Wood of the Picea abies, native to northern Europe and used as a timber and ornamental tree.

Machine-made laces produced in Nottingham, England. In order to carry this designation, the lace may be of any variety of patterns, but it must be knitted, twisted or embroidered in the United Kingdom by a member of the British Lace Federation. The term originally referred to lace produced on the Nottingham-lace machines, which was an invention that was originally used primarily for coarse lace production, employing larger bobbins than other lace machines and using pattern threads that were wound independently on section spools.
The color of this marble ranges from yellow, light brown, and pink to deep red. It originates not from Numidia proper, but from the mountains of Algeria and Mauritania.
A nontarnishing alloy typically used for making jewelry, which exhibits the natural color of gold. It is composed of copper (90%), aluminum (7.5%) and gold (2.5%).

Halbtransparente Flüssigkeit aus der Gallenblase von Rindern gewonnen und als Wässerungsmittel in Aquarellen verwendet. Ochsengalle enthält natriumchloride Salze (Taurocholate und Glycochloate) mit etwas Cholesterin, Lecithin und anderen Naturprodukten. Ochsengalle wird getrocknet und pulverisiert, dann zur Verwendung mit Wasser oder Ethanol gemischt. Es ist ein wirksames, natürliches Wässerungsmittel, das in der Aquarellmalerei verwendet wird, um die Geschmeidigkeit zu erleichtern. Ochsengalle wird auch in Marmorierungsarbeiten, bei der Gravur in der Lithographie und Textilfärbung eingesetzt. Es wurde als Weichmacher in Klebepasten verwendet.
Wood of the tree belonging to the genus Aesculus glabra.
Broad category of bobbin lace made in 16th and 17th century Flanders.
Wood of the species Quercus garryana, native to the western coast of North America, particularly common in the Willamette Valley. It has a compact texture and a straight grain.
Dünnes, leichtes, steifes, halbtransparentes Baumwollgewebe. Es ist ein einfach gewebter Stoff aus Kammgarnen.

Transparentes, glänzendes Gewebe in lockerer Leinwandbindung, das in der Regel aus Seide oder synthetischen Stoffen wie Rayon, Nylon, Acryl oder Polyester hergestellt wird. Es ist fein bis leicht, fest, stabil und haltbar. Es unterscheidet sich von Organdy, der aus Baumwolle hergestellt wird.
Wood of the species Fagus orientalis, native to Eurasia.
A blue pigment composed of Prussian blue mixed with synthetic ultramarine blue.
Wood from species Maclura pomifera; a hard, bright orange-colored wood that yields a dye.
Das Kollagen in Knochen; verwendet zur Leim- und Gelatineherstelleung.
Kalzuim-bindendes Protein in Knochen.
Wood of Abies amabilis, an ornamental and timber fir native to North America.

Kleines Dekorelement aus glänzendem Metall oder Kunststoff in jeglicher Form, in der Regel jedoch rund, mit einem Loch in der Mitte, das zur Befestigung an der Kleidung dient, um dekorative Effekte zu erzielen.
Refers to the material woven from the fiber of Pandanus leaves, used traditionally, for example, as sails for canoes constructed on the Society Islands of the South Pacific.
General term referring to any of several species of mahogany from South and Central America. Was mistakenly called "cedar" by Jesuit missionaries.
Similar to Keene's cement with borax instead of alum as a setting accelerator.
A pure white marble with transparent and flaky crystals; quarried on the island of Paros in the Cyclades, it was used by the ancient Greeks for sculpture as well as architecture.
An inferior grade of ivory black.

Type of bobbin lace made in Paris from the 18th century onwards. This firm lace, not fit for gathering, is characterized by its hexagonal mesh, which is formed by intertwining triangles. The pattern consists mostly of flowers, leaves or vines or occasionally animal figures.

Bezieht sich auf Glas, das durch eine Technik erzeugt worden ist, bei der gemahlenes Glas zu einer Paste verarbeitet und zu einem Gefäß oder einer Figur geformt wurde.
