Anschließend wurde es durch Gravur ausgearbeitet. Glas, das auf diese Art hergestellt wurde, war in der Antike bekannt. Im Frankreich des späten 19. und frühen 20. Jahrhunderts wurde diese Methode wiederbelebt, insbesondere im Studio des Glas- und Schmuck-Designers René Lalique.

Feines, dünnes Baumwoll- oder Leinentuch, das ein- oder beidseitig mit Stärke oder einer Stärkemischung bezogen ist und vor allen Dingen von Architekten und Designern für das Erstellen von Pausen  mit Tusche oder Bleistift verwendet wurde.

A dark grayish blue or gray pigment mixture generally composed of ivory black with small amounts of white and blue, namely synthetic ultramarine blue, Prussian blue, or phthalocyanine blue. The pigment is used for watercolors.
Der Mantel aus Wolle, der ein Schaf oder ein ähnliches Tier umgibt.

A famous Greek marble quarried at Mount Pentelikon near Athens. It is pure white but may turn yellow after long exposure to air; a few miniscule veins of talc sometimes cause a faint greenish tint. It was used in antiquity as early as the 6th century BCE and continued to be popular for both sculpture and architecture; both the sculptural decoration and the architectural members of the Parthenon are made of Pentelic marble.
Feiner, leichter Canvas aus Baumwolle, der in der Regel mit einem Glanz versehen und einfarbig gefärbt ist, vor allem zum Buchbunden und als Futterstoff verwendet.
Harte, perlenartige schillernde Innenschicht verschiedener Muschelschalen, die häufig zur Herstellung von kleinen Gegenständen und Einlegearbeiten verwendet wird.
Perlon is a type of polyamide created by the German company Bayer-Faser in the 1940s.
Lake pigment extracted from small berries from various a buckthorn  plant, Rhamnus infectorius.
Close-grained, strong wood of the species Parrotia persica, a small tree native to Iran.

Named after the first-century BCE physician Philon of Tarsus. To relieve pain, induce sleep, prevent miscarriages, and reduce the pain of hemorrhoids and of heavy menstruation, pharmacists offered this drug, made from such ingredients as opium, saffron, white pepper, pearls, and amber.
An unstandardized name for many red pigments, included but not limited to Indian red, Persian Gulf oxide, light red, and a variety of chrome red colors.

A dark brown oleoresin obtained from Myroxylon balsamum var. pereirae trees that are native to the coastlines of Central America. Peru balsam contains high amounts of cinnamic acid, benzoic acid, and vanillin. It is used for perfumes and flavorings. Peru balsam has also been used as a dark brown glaze for paintings. Peru balsam is very similar to Tolu balsam, dervied from a variety of the same species. A dark brown oleoresin obtained from Myroxylon balsamum var. pereirae trees that are native to the coastlines of Central America. Peru balsam contains high amounts of cinnamic acid, benzoic acid, and vanillin. It is used for perfumes and flavorings. Peru balsam has also been used as a dark brown glaze for paintings. Peru balsam is very similar to Tolu balsam, dervied from a variety of the same species.
Haar von der Mähne und dem Schweif der Pferde, vor allem für die Pinselherstellung verwendet, aber auch für die Bögen von Musikinstrumenten oder zur Herstellung von Haartüchern.

Feste, strapazierfähige Textilie, die sich durch Schnüreffekte entweder in der Kette oder im Schuss auszeichnet, ursprünglich aus weißer Baumwolle, später aus Kunstseide oder Seide, bedruckt oder farbig; wird genutzt für Kleider, Blusen, Hosen, Sportbekleidung, Täschchen und Halsbekleidung.
A paste of wood flour or wood cellulose compounded with a synthetic resin used for filling cavities or seams in wood products.
Ein geschnittener Samt mit sehr langem Flor.

Bezeichnet eine Art Venezianische Spitze, die charakterisiert ist durch den Einschluss von vielen leichten Faden-Stegen oder -Bügeln, die mit Motiven wie Schlingen, Ösen und Sternen, die Schneeflocken ähneln, gearbeitet sind.

Bezeichnet eine Art Spitze, die dem “punto a relievo” ähnelt, sich von diesem unterscheidet, indem sie weniger pompös, aber mit mehr Ornament erscheint. Sie ist charakterisiert durch den Einschluss vieler kleiner Maschen und Rosetten.

Bezeichnet ein frühes Beispiel von Venezianischer Spitze, charakterisiert durch eine relativ flache Oberfläche. Spätere Venezianische Spitze besitzt ein stark eingeschnittenes oder erhabenes Erscheinungsbild.

Term used in the glass trade for a type of art glass made by the New England Glass Company in 1885, consisting of clear glass enlarged by repeated mold blowing and decorated with a network of acid etched lines, or having a mottled surface created by rolling the vessel in an acid resistant substance and then acid bathing.

General term referring to a modern blue pigment that is chemically similar to Egyptian blue, but tends to be purer and finer than the ancient pigment, or to commercial products containing a dye of the same hue.
An unstandardized name used for Pozzuoli red, Tuscan red, and dragon's blood.

African textile material of Ewe origin but adapted and varied by other peoples, woven on a loom in narrow strips to produce strong patterns. A popular cloth, it is traded all around the Niger Delta region.
Wood of the Cupressus lawsoniana from the West coast of the United States. Timber is white in color with streaks of yellow and traces of red, and is used for doors, boats, matches, and patterns.

A purplish red and white clouded stone which may come from either the island of Iasus or the island of Chios. It was supposedly favored by the emperor Claudius in antiquity and was later used in the door jambs of the Porta Santa of St. Peter's in Rome, hence its name.
Stone of English origin consisting of fossils cemented together with lime.
An oolitic limestone widely used for building in England, particularly in London; it is quarried on the Isle of Portland, off the coast of England.
Kunstvolle Verzierung bei Möbeln oder Kleindung, wie zum Beispiel, Tressen, Kordeln, Fransen, Gimpen oder Quasten, oft kombiniert.

A striking breccia quarried in the Blue Ridge Mountains in Maryland and Virginia; it consists mostly of limestone, quartz pebbles, and multicolored fragments ranging from sand grains to cobbles, all cemented together in a calcareous matrix. It can be extremely difficult to work as the hard pebbles tend to break away from the softer matrix.

A siliceous sandstone, from Saint Lawrence County in New York, containing a small amount of iron oxide, giving it its red or reddish-brown color. Although soft enough to work economically when first quarried, it becomes incredibly hard upon exposure, making it an incredibly durable building stone.
General term referring to a modern blue pigment that is chemically similar to Egyptian blue.

A bright, natural red volcanic pigment originally mined at Pozzuoli near Naples, Italy. It was used by the Romans and others as a paint pigment, becoming popular for fresco paintings because it dried quickly to a hard impenetrable surface like cement.

Refers to an alloy used in the jewelry and watch trade during the 17th and 18th centuries, mostly in England; it was golden in color and was probably an alloy of copper and zinc that resembled brass. Some sources specifically describe it as a type of brass with a composition of three parts copper to one of zinc pickled in a diluted spirit of vitriol and then immersed in aquafortis before burnishing. Prince's metal was supposedly invented by Prince Rupert of the Rhine.

A coarse variegated marble, quarried on the island of Proconnesus, present-day Marmara Adasi, Turkey, that is either gray, white, or white with gray stripes. Large quantities of this marble were imported to Greece to be carved into sarcophagi. Also called Cyzican marble because it was used extensively in Cyzicus, present-day Belkis, as it was in Constantinople as well.

Any of several deep blue pigments consisting chiefly of ferric ferrocyanide; first developed in Berlin in 1704 by Diesbach. It is made by adding ferric chloride to a boiling solution of hexacyano ferrate. Prussian blue has deep blue, finely divided particles that are transparent in watercolors; it has high tinting strength and is stable to light, although it turns brown in the presence of alkalis or heat. It is used as a colorant in cyanotypes, blueprint paper, laundry blue, linoleum, leather, plastics, paper, cosmetics, and formerly as a textile dye for silk, cotton, and wool, where it was mordanted with ferric oxide.

A green pigment originally prepared by the nearly the same process as Prussian blue, but produced by omitting the hydrochloric acid step that converted the green to blue. Beginning in the mid-19th century, Prussian green sold commercially as a mixture of Prussian blue and a yellow pigment.

Bezeichnet eine Arbeit aus Faden oder Schnur, die verknotet, verwickelt oder geschnürt hergestellt wird. Sie entwickelte sich in mediterranen Gebieten, wahrscheinlich aus dem Fischernetz. Besonders verbreitet war sie im 16. Jahrhundert in Italien, wo dekorative Säume gefertigt wurden, indem geknoten, gewickelt und geschnürt wurde, ohne Gewichte oder Klöppel zu verwenden. Frühe Muster waren geometrisch, manchmal mit schematischen Figuren. Man nimmt an, dass Klöppel- oder Kissenspitze aus diesem Verfahren entwickelt wurde, indem die Fäden mit Bleigewichten versehen wurden und das Muster auf einem Polster oder Kissen befestigt wurde.

Bezeichnet eine Sorte von Spitze, die aus der flachen Venezianischen Spitze entwickelt wurde. Sie ist charakterisiert durch ein erhabenes Muster, das durch umrissene Schnörkel- oder Blattmuster mit einem Cordonnet-Faden, einem relativ starken Faden, oder mit gebündelten Fäden gefertigt wird; das Muster wird dann mit Lochstich überarbeitet, so dass die Schnörkel und Blätter in einem erhabenen Relief erscheinen.

Stone quarried in the vicinity of the Isle of Purbeck peninsula, Dorset, England; used primarily as a building stone. It is actually a hard semi-metamorphosed limestone formed from the shells of snails. Given that the stone is hard enough to take a fine polish it is called "Purbeck marble," even though it is not fully metamophosed marble.
A low-expansion type of bora-silicate glass.

Angehängter Besatz, Schmuck oder Kleidungsteil, das aus losen Fäden besteht. Vor dem 20 Jahrhundert eine Holzform, die mit Seiden- oder Kammgarnfäden bedeckt wurde; heute oft nur noch ein Bündel von Fäden, Kordeln oder anderen Strängen, die an der Spitze zusammenlaufen.

A light brown or buff-colored dolostone that is well-cemented, free of jointing, and ideally suited for building purposes. It is often used as a building stone in Toronto, Canada as it is quarried in southern Ontario; Queenston limestone caps the Niagara Escarpment. Before a quarry operation was begun at Queenston in about 1840, Scottish settlers exploited the limestone beds for building materials; the present quarry is one of the oldest in Canada.

Kleines, mit Vellum durchsetztes Quadrat aus Gold, in Stapeln angeordnet und mit Pergamentstreifen gebunden, das dann in der ersten Phase der manuellen Herstellung von Goldblättern gehämmert wird, bevor es in die "Lotform" überführt wird.
Ein Präparat, das schäumt und auf einer Oberfläche aufgetragen wird, um den Bereich für die Rasur weich zu machen und vorzubereiten.
Köper, bei dem die Richtung der durch die Bindungspunkte gebildeten Diagonalen über Gruppen von Enden und auch über Gruppen von Schüssen umgekehrt wird, um ein gewebtes Rautenmuster zu erzeugen.
Calcareous sandstone quarried in the vicinity of Reigate, Surrey, England; used primarily as a building stone.
Dense wood of the Rimu conifer tree, native to New Zealand. The sapwood is pale and susceptible to pests;  the heartwood is highly prized for its durability and aesthetic.
Any of a class of nucleic acid containing ribose and uracil involved in translating the genetic information inscribed in DNA to the cell’s protein manufacturing sites.
A jet black marble with irregular white veins, occurring in Virginia, that is considered the best American black marble.
Brick whose nominal dimensions are 2 2/3 x 4 x 12 inches.
Refers to a type of travertine that is beige, cream, honey, and/or gold in color; it is lighter in color than barco.
Term occasionally used to refer to hand-woven Italian linen from the 17th and 18th centuries.
Nonstandardized name for a yellow earth pigment, typically having a warm yellow color, generally from a material produced in Italy and used in ancient Rome.
Ancient pewter containing 70% tin and 30% lead, with other elements such as iron present as impurities.
Reddish-brown pigment originally prepared with sepia blended with alizarin crimson or burnt sienna.
A channel-shaped, tapered, single lap, roofing tile.
A durable, dense, pink dolerite granite from quarries in and around Porriiño, Galicia, Spain.
Trade name for a large and varied line of textured boards that are similar to coquille boards.
A orange brown transparent pigment made from synthetic alizarin.
Material, das an der Rückseite eines Buchblockes verwendet wird, um den Rücken zu verstärken; es wird auch verwendet, um die Innenseite des Deckels entlang des Buchrückens auszukleiden.

Streifen aus Spitze, Stoff, Leder oder einem anderen Material, der an der Kante gerafft oder schräg geschnitten ist, sodass er, wenn er am Saum, an der Knopfleiste, am Halsausschnitt oder Ärmelabschluss eines Kleidungsstückes angebracht ist, eine Zierkrause oder ein Volant bildet.

Red or reddish-brown calf originally produced in Russia and introduced into western Europe in the 17th century, vegetable-tanned with willow, poplar, or larch bark and curried on the flesh side with extract from birch bark, which gave it a characteristic pleasing odor thought to repel insects. It was often finished with a diced pattern. Between 1780 and 1830 it was highly prized for bookbinding, "Russia leather" was made in imitation of it.

Vegetable-tanned leather made originally of shaved cowhide, later of calfskin, goatskin, sheepskin or horse hide, tanned with willow and other types of bark, then curried on the flesh side with birch bark extract to give it a pleasant but relatively short-lived odor in imitation of Russia calf.  Its uses included for upholstery and for binding blank books.
Lace produced in Russia and characterized by a coiling tape forming elegant patterns in very simple to very elaborate designs.
Yarn in which the twist of its component fiber follows the direction of the central bar of the letter S.

Weißlich-gelbliche Schicht, die auf der Milch schwimmt; sie bestehend aus 18 bis 40 Prozent Butterfett. Weißlich-gelbliche Schicht, die auf der Milch schwimmt; sie besteht aus 18 bis 40 Prozent Butterfett.
A deep blue-black marble with short white veins that is found in Saint Anne, Belgium.
Glänzendes Seidengewebe, das manchmal mit Gold- oder Silberfäden verwoben war und vor allem im Mittelalter für die Kleidung von Geistlichen und Adeligen verwendet wurde.

Kettsamt, in der Regel aus Seide, mit einem kurzen, weichem dichten Flor, der durch eine zusÃ¤tzliche Kette erzeugt wird, die durch das EinfÃ¼hren von StÃ¤ben wÃ¤hrend des Webens in die Schlingen Ã¼ber die OberflÃ¤che des Stoffs gehoben wird; die Schlingen kÃ¶nnen geschnitten oder ungeschnitten sein.

Tree bark employed historically as writing material for important manuscripts in Assam, India. To write on Sanchipat, a waterproof ink was used, prepared with Silikha seed, Kehraj (a type of grass),ashes and minerals and applied with a bird’s feather. The material and associated technique are now rarely used.
A variegated French breccia marble, usually with gray, red, or yellow predominating, quarried near Sarrancolin in the Pyrenees.

A hydrated form of copper arsenite, formerly used as a pigment in calico printing and wallpaper manufacture, warm yellowish-green in color with good opacity, but never widely used as a paint pigment because it is toxic and discolors in the presence of acid or sulfur fumes. Currently, copper arsenite is used as a rodenticide, insecticide, fungicide, and wood preservative. Scheele's green was discovered in Sweden in 1775 by Carl W. Scheele, a German chemist, but he did not publish the recipe until 1778.

Material aus dünn geschnittenen Platten der flachen Hornschuppen des Panzers einiger Wasserschildkröten oder Landschildkröten. Schildpatt war im 19. Jahrhundert als Material für Intarsien, Schmuck, Haarkämme und andere dekorative Objekte oder Verzierungen beliebt, aber seine Verwendung ist heute verboten.
Bezeichnet den Schnabel von Vögeln oder ähnliche verhornte Mundpartien anderer Tiere.
An early variety of chromium oxide green.
Eines der langen, schützenden Haare oder Borsten, die um das Maul der Tiere wächst.
Stein, oft Granit, Kalkstein oder Diabas, der zerschlagen, zerkleinert und sortiert wurde und dann zur Herstellung von Beton, Gleisschotter und beim Straßenbau verwendet wird.
Feuchtigkeit. die durch die Poren der Haut der meisten Säugetiere ausgeschieden wird, meist als Folge von körperlicher Anstrengung oder Wärme, um den Körper zu kühlen.

Wood from the Pinus sylvestris, native to Eurasia and imported to North America. The Scotch pine has tough, light brown wood. Its lumber is used for millwork and it is an important conifer for timber in Europe.
Brick wider and longer than most other brick; used chiefly for garden walls.
Textilie aus Seide, einer Faser, die aus dem Kokon des Seidenspinners gewonnen wird.

A fine-grained red, reddish-brown, and gray sandstone from Seneca Creek, Maryland; it contains coarse- to fine-grained angular quartz as well as some feldspar and mica fragments. It is fairly easy to carve but darkens and hardens when exposed to air, making it one of the most durable building stones. Seneca sandstone was extremely popular in Washington, D.C. during the 'brownstone era' of about 1840 to 1880; the original Smithsonian Institution building, for example, is made of Seneca sandstone.

Strapazierfähiger Köper aus Kammgarn oder mit Kette aus Kammgarn und Schuss aus einem anderen Wollgarn, mit einer klaren Oberfläche und einem flachen diagonalen Grat; wird häufig für maßgeschneiderte Kleidungsstücke wie Anzüge, Mäntel und Kleider verwendet.

The blue used by the Seri Indians of Sonora Mexico in face painting. It is a prepared pigment derived from root bark of Franseria dumosa which is ground with a light-colored local clay and green organic resin of the flowering plant Guaiacum coulteri.
Ein Präparat zum Reinigen, insbesondere für Haar oder Stoff.
A pinkish-red and gray English granite that is distinguished by embedded crystals of red felspar, sometimes as long as 2 inches. There are 2 distinct shades, Light Shap and Dark Shap.
Plate consisting of an overlay of silver on copper, brass, or other metal so as to simulate solid silver.
Wood of species Larix sibirica.
Wood of the species Fagus crenata, similar to European beech with cream-colored sapwood and brown heartwood. It is used for tool handles, flooring, joinery, plywood, and furniture.

Yellow and light brown marble quarried near Siena, Italy that is sought after for interior architectural use, both in the past and in the present; it is compact and often brecciated, with a waxy lustre.

A medium-hard, pale yellow variety of copal resin obtained from trees of the genus Copaifera native to Sierra Leone, Guinea, and Ghana. It has been used in varnishes. A medium-hard, pale yellow variety of copal resin obtained from trees of the genus Copaifera native to Sierra Leone, Guinea, and Ghana. It has been used in varnishes.

Dammars shipped under the tradename "Singapore" from the island of Singapore. Singapore dammar is paler than Batavian dammar and has less tendency to bloom. Dammars shipped under the tradename "Singapore" from the island of Singapore. Singapore dammar is paler than Batavian dammar and has less tendency to bloom.

Eine dicke, klebrige Flüssigkeit, gewöhnlich aus einer konzentrierten Zuckerlösung in Wasser oder in einem anderen Träger, wie Fruchtsaft. Genutzt als Nahrung, beim Kochen oder als Trägerstoff für ein Arzneimittel.

Wood from Picea sitchensis, found along the coast of western North America. It is soft, pale reddish brown color wood with a straight grain. The stiff wood is used for millwork, boxes, musical instruments, boats, airplanes, ladders, and paper pulp.
Solution of hydrochloric acid and potassium chlorate used as a mordant in aquatint.
An exceedingly fine and even-bedded magnesian limestone, thinly stratified, quarried in the vicinity of Solnhofen, Bavaria, Germany; widely used in lithography.

Wood of the species Swietenia macrophylla, native to areas in southern North America, Central and South America. It has been introduced for cultivation in Asia. The wood is used for cabinet-making and other purposes.
A paste used to fill holes and cracks in plaster, wallboard, wood, or other surfaces.
Non-standardized name for various organic black pigments, including vine black, cork black, and others.
Wood of Cedrela odorata (a mahogany, not true cedar) used in the United States for cigar boxes, furniture, carving, and interior trim.
A dull reddish brown earth pigment known to have been used in inexpensive 18th-century paints, as well as in other materials.
Marbled paper with light and dark waves that diagonally cross the marbled pattern. Occasionally the paper is folded or creased in squares or diamond shapes before staining to increase the effect.
An earth pigment containing red iron oxide, originally obtained from Spain. It is a coarse grain, an inexpensive colorant, having a dull red color, and used in industrial paints.
Clay roofing tile, approximately semicylindrical in shape; laid in cornices with the units having their convex sides alternately up and down.
Viskoses, farbloses, leicht opales Sekret, das ist in der Regel in Reaktion leicht alkalisch ist und Wasser, Mucin, Eiweiß, Salz und oft stärkespaltende Enzyme enthält.

Milchprodukt, hergestellt aus Rahm, Aromen und Zucker, während des Einfrieren langsam gerührt, um die Bildung großer Eiskristalle zu verhindern und eine gleichmäßige, halbfeste Masse entstehen lässt. Milchprodukt, hergestellt aus Rahm, Aromen und Zucker, die während des Einfrierens langsam gerührt werden, um die Bildung großer Eiskristalle zu verhindern, sodass eine gleichmäßige, halbfeste Masse entsteht.

Bezieht sich auf eine feine, durchbrochene Textilarbeit mit Ornamenten, deren Entwürfe oder Muster durch Verknoten, Verschlingen, Verdrehen, Verflechten oder Verschnüren von Fäden aus Leinen, Baumwolle, Silber, Haaren, Metall oder anderem Material entstehen. Spitze kann mit Nadeln oder mit Klöppeln hergestellt werden. Stickereien können hinzugefügt werden. Moderne Spitze kann maschinell gefertigt sein. Durchbrucharbeiten, die an einem Webstuhl entstanden sind oder ornamentale gestrickte Arbeiten werden generell nicht als Spitze bezeichnet. Spitze ist oft weiß oder einfarbig. Echte Spitze wurde im 14. Jahrhundert in Europa und dem Nahen Osten entwickelt, obwohl es auch in antiken Kulturen, wie in Ägypten, gemusterte Durchbrucharbeiten gab. Spitze kann als Borte oder Rand verwendet werden oder auf Leinen oder Kleidung befestigt; als große Stücke wird sie für Behänge, Tuchwaren, Kleidung oder andere Gegenstände verwendet.
Hohler, spitzer Stacheln eines Stachelschweins, Ameisenigels oder Igels. Findet Verwendungin der indianischen Ornamentstickerei und für andere dekorative Zwecke.

A siliceous sandstone from Derbyshire, England; it is also considered a gritstone, specifically a Darley Dale gritstone. It is even-grained and exceedingly hard, making it an excellent choice for ashlar and paving work. Its silica content is so high, however, that trade unions have been known to ban its use owing to the danger of silicosis; whenever possible, Stancliffe sandstone is now only worked by wet methods.

Allgemeine Bezeichnung für ein Gestein, das geschnitten, geformt, zerteilt oder anderweitig geformt wurde, zur Verwendung im Bau oder für andere Zwecke. Umfasst auch einzelne Objekte aus Stein im spezifischen archäologischen oder anthropologischen Sinn, die dekoriert oder verziert  sein können und bei rituellen Zeremonien Verwendung fanden. Diese Steine sind in der Regel nicht behauen oder bearbeitet und unterschieden sich daher von Steinskulpturen.
Marbled paper prepared with a turpentine mix, as well as the added oil or gall. The color then breaks into fine holes.
Großer, hervorstehender Zahn, wie bei Elefanten und Walrössern, in erster Linie aus Dentin bestehend.

An aromatic, thick, viscous exudation obtained from several Central European fir trees, including Abies alba. Strasbourg turpentine contains about 57% resinous acids, 28% terpenes, and 13% resins. It is similar to Venice turpentine and has been used since the 16th century as an additive to oil paints, mastic, and other varnishes.

A shell marble from Sussex, England that is predominantly gray with occasional green or blue tinges. Its old local name, 'winklestone,' refers to the abundance of small snail shells (winkles) embedded in the marble. Used to ornament churches in the Middle Ages, it remained popular for centuries.
Marbled paper made in Sweden and having a characteristic spotted pattern.
Onyx marble quarried in the vicinity of Tabriz, North Western Iran. It is used primarily as a building stone.

Steifer Stoff, in der Regel aus Leinwandbindung, manchmal mit einer feinen horizontalen Rippung und einer glatten, glänzenden Oberfläche auf beiden Seiten, ursprünglich aus Seide, heute aus unterschiedlichen Fasern.

Among the most important types of rock used for garden building in China.  A type of limestone categorized according to its northern and southern varieties, it is found both underwater and in the mountains.  The type found in Lake Taihu in Jiangsu province is the most well-known variety, and thus “Taihu rock” has become a general designation for such stones.
Wood of the species Sassafras radaiense, native to Taiwan.

Durchsichtige, dünne, stark appretierte weit gewebte Baumwolle, die für Futterstoffe oder als Wischgaze in der Druckerei verwendet wird. Der Begriff findet auch Verwendung für eine Art von Musselin, der im 18. Jahrhundert aus Indien in das Vereinigte Königreich gebracht und im 19. Jahrhundert in Schottland vor allem für Ballkleider genutzt wurde.

Stoff, der aus verschiedenfarbigen Garnen gewebt ist, um ein mehrfarbiges Karomuster, oft aus Woll- oder Kammgarnköper. Obwohl es diese Art von Stoffmuster in vielen frühen Kulturen gab, gehören die schottischen Tartans zu den bekanntesten, deren charakteristische Muster und Farben einen Hochland-Clan kennzeichneten und nur von Mitgliedern dieses Clans getragen wurden.
A coarse, variegated marble from Hawkins County, Tennessee. It is dark chocolate brown or red in color with white streaks and masses throughout and it usually contains some fossils.

A fine pink marble, suitable for sculpture, of which a number of varieties exist. Tennessee marbles are generally harder and more compact than other American marbles, making them more suitable for outdoor use but also making them more difficult to carve and polish.

Ein gebranntes oder niedrig-gebranntes Material, das gewöhnlich eine Mischung aus Lehm, Schamottemehl und Wasser ist. Es wird seit der Antike für Töpferwaren, Statuetten, Lampen, Dachziegel und Gesimse verwendet. Es kann vor dem Brennen glasiert werden. Um einen Gegenstand herzustellen, wird Terrakotta geformt, mehrere Tage lang getrocknet und dann bei mindestens 600 °C gebrannt. Es ist feuerfest, leichter als Stein und hat in der Regel eine bräunlich-rote Farbe.

A coarse-textured white marble that is nearly pure calcium carbonate; it was used to build the first 152 feet of the Washington Monument in Washington, DC. It is quarried in Texas, Maryland, not in the state of Texas.

Dense wood of the species Diospyros texana, native to Texas and Oklahoma in the United States, and northeastern Mexico. The sapwood is yellow, while the heartwood found in large trees is ebony black. It is used for furniture and printing blocks, among other purposes.

An explosive chemical used in munitions and demolition. Initially used in the dye industry, it was not used as an explosive until 1904. It can be melted and cast due to the large difference between its melting point and decomposition temperature, and insensitivity to shock. It will not explode without a detonator.

Human remains comprising preserved heads of Māori indigenous people of New Zealand, valued because the faces had been decorated by tā moko tattooing. The remains were valuable trade items during the Musket Wars of the early 19th century. Many of the remains were repatriated to Te Papa Tongarewa in 2014.

Refers to a bobbin-made lace that is characerized by simple geometric patterns and relatively coarse designs. It typically made by peasants in many European countries; it is noted as one of the mainstays of the lace makers of the Austrian Tirol and as the type of lace on which apprentices generally begin learning lace-making.

Durch den Tränenapparat ausgeschiede Flüssigkeit. Tränen, zusammen mit anderen Sekreten helfen den schützenden  Tränenfilm auf der Außenseite des Auges zu bilden. Risse können in Reaktion auf eine Vielzahl von Reizen reflektorisch (helle Lichter usw.) ausgeglichen werden. Die Absonderung von Tränen als Folge emotionaler Erregung wird als psychisches Weinen bezeichnet.

Eine schmaler Besatz, der durch verschiedene Techniken wie Brettchenweben oder Flechten hergestellt wird. Es gibt sie in einer Vielzahl von Fasern und Gewichten, aber er ist schwerer als Bänder und flacher als Kordeln.
Netzgewebe aus durchsichtiger Seide, Nylon oder Kunstseide mit einer sechseckigen Netzstruktur, genutzt für Hüte, Schleier, Ballet- und Damenkleider.

Goatskin leather imported from Turkey through Venice to Europe where it was used in fine bookbinding from the early 16th to the early 18th century. It was tawed with oil before the hair was removed, stained a distinctive shade of red, then tanned with sumac.
An unstandardized hue name for either a madder obtained from the Near East or any synthetic colorant of the same hue.
Marbled paper showing black veins with orange, yellow and white portions. The dominant color is applied last, which pushes the other colors into fine veins.

An inorganic blue pigment that is a complex ferric ferricyanide that is produced by reacting a ferrous salt with potassium ferricyanide, produced as an intermediate in the production of Prussian blue but not the same compound. Turnbull's blue is not used as a paint pigment, but in making blueprints. Blueprint paper is sensitized with ferric ammonium citrate and potassium ferricyanide. When the paper is exposed to light, the ferric ions are reduced to ferrous ions.

A strong yellow pigment made by mixing powdered lead chloride with lead carbonate, then fusing. This yellow pigment was used for a short time in oil paints, but its sensitivity to light and sulfur fumes limited its use and it was replaced by chrome yellow pigments.
A bright red pigment prepared by depositing an organic red dye, such as alizarin, on a red iron oxide base.
Raue, gebräunte Seide, hergestellt von den wilden Seidenraupen aus China und Indien; die Faser ist gröber, stärker und kürzer als die Seidenfaser aus kultivierter Produktion.
German utensil alloy, contains 88% to 92% tin, up to 3% copper, and 6% to 7.5% lead.
Gewebe aus Wolle mit einer rauhen Oberfläche, das wie selbst gesponnen wirkt.

Purple or reddish dye historically made by the ancient Phoenicians using mucus from the hypobranchial gland of either of two species of sea snails.This dye is a rare animal-produced organobromine compound, which the snails make using a specific bromoperoxidase enzyme that operates on dissolved bromide in sea water. Used in Greek and Roman times for dyeing togas and in the Middle Ages for decorating manuscripts. The mollusks secrete a yellowish liquid that oxidizes to a purplish red in bright sunlight; each mollusk only produces one drop of colorant so large numbers were needed to produce enough dye for an entire cloth, making the dye was very expensive.
Commercial name for a permanent, bluish-green earth pigment mined near Tirol.

Klare, bernsteinfarbene Flüssigkeit (Säugetiere, Wasserlebewesen) oder graue, halbfeste Ausscheidung (Vögel, Reptilien) als Stoffwechselschlacken von Wirbeltieren. Die Substanz wird bei Säugetieren in der Niere gebildet. Sie besteht aus einer Reihe von wasserlöslichen Nebenprodukten des Eiweißstoffwechsel (Harnstoff, Kreatinin, Harnsäure, Ammoniak, etc.), vermischt mit Salzen und Pigmenten. Urin wird bei der Reinigung, dem Färben und Gerben verwendet.
Lace originally manufactured in the town of Valenciennes, in northern France.

A naturally occurring dark brown earth pigment containing organic humus, peat, or coal material mixed with iron oxides, alumina and silica. When ignited, the pigment leaves a soft gray residue. The colorant is fugitive and fades on exposure to strong light. The pigment was first used in the 17th century; early sources were from the Cologne and Kassel regions of Germany, thus the alternate pigment names. The brown earth has since been obtained from various localities each of which may differ slightly in color and composition. To add to this confusion some pigments labeled "Van Dyck brown" were bituminous while others were synthetically made from from carbon black and iron oxide mixtures. For these synthetic mixtures, use "synthetic Van Dyck brown."

Refers to distinctive glass made in Venice, Italy. It can refer to early glass made from about 450 CE when glass-makers from Aquileia fled there and were soon joined by others from Byzantium. In addition, the term is particularly used to refer to glass made on the Venetian island of Murano from before 1292 to the present day. It is generally a sodiac type of glass; soda glass is light in weight and highly ductile. Venetian glass incorporates a number of styles and techniques although Venetian glass-makers particularly excelled in the making of colored glass, agate glass, opaque white glass (lattimo), cristallo, filigrana, and millefiori. Engraving is rarely found while enameling and gilding are common, particularly in fifteenth- and sixteenth-century specimens. In order to protect their profits and the secrets of Venetian glass, the Venetians confined glassmakers under pain of death to the island of Murano; however, the techniques were eventually imitated in "Altare glass" and "façon de Venise." For glass made exclusively on Murano, see "Murano glass."

Refers to needle lace created in Venice primarily during the 16th through 19th centuries. It also refers to imitations of Venetian lace made later and/or in other locations. It is characterized by a sumptuousness defined by the large size of the pieces of lace and by the rich high-relief patterns and Baroque designs. Early examples were created by stitching together sections of deep, acute-angled points of lacework, which were typically used for making ruffs and collars known as "vandykes."  Later designs were more curvilinear. Lace making declined in Venice proper in the early 19th century, but lace is still made on the nearby island of Burano.

A permanent, reddish brown pigment that was originally prepared from a natural red ocher, but since the 18th century has been manufactured by calcining ferrous sulfate (copperas) with lime or calcium carbonate in a ratio of around 15-40% ferric oxide and 60-80% calcium sulfate. It is used in oil paints, house paints, and as a paper colorant.

A thick, viscous exudation from the Austrian larch tree, Larix decidua, containing 63% resinous acids, 20% terpenes, and 14% resins. It has been used since the 16th century in small amounts to produce an enamel-like surface that is non-yellowing. However, it is not used as a varnish because it quickly becomes dark and brittle when exposed to oxygen and light. It is also used as a mounting medium for light microscopy and as a plasticizer in glue paste linings. Slate having a pale green color, quarried in Vermont.
A fine-grained white marble from Vermont that is highly valued by sculptors; it may contain bluish-gray veins or clouds.
An high quality pigment composed of green earth obtained from Cyprus; it was highly valued in the Middle Ages.
Dark red stone quarried in two deposits near Verona, in the Lessinia geographical area of the northern Veneto Prealps.

Pigment name for a variety of green pigments, originally referring to green created from a mixture of blue and yellow colors prepared by Paolo Veronese, a 16th century Venetian painter. It is also used for any pigment resembling that color, including green earth pigments, copper-based emerald green, viridian, and other pigments of similar color.

An unstandardized term for various green pigments, including malachite green dye, pigment mixtures of lead chromate and Prussian blue, mixtures of zinc yellow and Prussian blue, of zinc chrome green and hydrated chrome oxide, of viridian and zinc yellow, and a ceramic color that is chemically the same as vernalis.

A wine-colored organic colorant from various sources; originally made with cochineal or brazilwood dye. In the 19th century, aniline dyes were used to make Vienna lake. None of the colorants are permanent and are usually restricted to interior house paints.
Trademarked name for a vinyl resin used in the production of phonograph records.
Refers to thick homogeneous opaque structural glass used especially for ornamental finish on surfaces exposed to the weather.
Leichter, versteifter epidermaler Auswuchs, der die äußere Hülle der Körper von Vögeln bildet.
Weicher, leicht gedrehter Faserstrang, der vor der eigentlichen Verarbeitung zu Garn verstreckt und von Fremdstoffen befreit wurde
Mit Öl imprägnierter Stoff; oft aus Baumwolle, Jute oder Hanf gewebt und mit Öl und Pigmenten behandelt. Wird in der Regel als wasserdichte Abdeckung genutzt.

Ein weiches Kissen aus grobgefilzten Baumwoll- Polyester- oder Wollfasern, das oft in Form einer Rolle vorliegt und bei Polstermöbeln, für Kissen, Verpackung, zur Puppenherstellung, Polsterung und als Einlage in Steppdecken verwendet wird.
Oolitic limestone quarried in the vicinity of Weldon, Northhamptonshire, England; used primarily as a building stone.
Marbled paper produced usually with two colors, usually a light and dark of the same color. Similar to Spanish marbled paper, but without the wave design.
Wood from the Picea smithiana.

General term used by the lumber trade for firm, close-grained woods that resemble true boxwood but are derived from several other species of tree, including Phyllostylon brasiliensis, Tabebuia pentaphylla, and several species in the genera Eucalyptus and Tristania.

Lustrous, dark yellow wood from the species Zanthoxylum flavum, native to South America, the Caribbean islands, and Africa. This oily timber has a close, irregular grain, smells like coconut, and polishes to a high gloss.

Hard, fine-grained wood of the species Swietenia mahagoni, native to southern Florida, the Bahamas, Cuba, Jamaica, and Hispaniola. It turns reddish brown at maturity and takes a good polish. This is the species for which the term "mahogany" was first coined, derived from a Native American term. The wood is used for cabinet-making and other purposes.
A type of seam-face granite quarried in Weymouth, Massachusetts.
A white marble with black and gray blotches from Pickens County, Georgia; it is coarser than white marble from Vermont.
A gray granite native to Wicklow, Ireland.
A red and brown marble variegated with white dolomitic, quarried near Winooski, Vermont.
Limestone quarried in the US state of Wisconsin, known for a wide color range.
Textil aus [Wolle](300014074), dem spinnfähigen Haar von Säugetieren.
Bezeichnet den getrockneten Schweiß von Schafen, der sich in der Wolle ablagert.

A bluestone that is similar to Hudson bluestone and other bluestones from New York; it is characterized by a fine, compact texture and a dark blue-gray color. It is considered one of the most valuable stones found in Wyoming for building and flagging purposes.

Handmade paper from the bark of the Tara Wing-Celtis (Pteroceltis tatarinowii) or Blue Sandalwood tree and rice straw. It was originally and continues to be produced in Jing County, now in Anhui Province in eastern China. The county was under the jurisdiction of Xuanzhou Prefecture in the Tang Dynasty (618-907), hence its name. The paper is known for its strong, smooth surface, its ability to absorb water and moisten ink, and fold repeatedly without breaking. It has been widely used in calligraphy, painting and book printing, and increasingly in conservation work. In the western world, it has at times been characterized as rice paper, although wood bark is traditionally its main ingredient with rice straw added.
Yarn in which the twist of its component fibers follows the direction of the central bar of the letter Z.
Verfestigtes, mineralisiertem oder partiell mineralisiertem Gewebe, die in den Mündern von Lebenwesen gebildet werden; zu unterscheiden von anderen Knochen durch seine Struktur.
Eine Paste zum Reinigen der Zähne.

Sehr feste, kalkhaltige Substanz zur Ummantelung von Zahnkonen oder ganzen Zähnen bei Säugern. Zahnschmelz bei erwachsenen Tieren wird überwiegend aus Apatitkristallen gebildet, die aus Kalzium und Phosphat bestehen. Zahnschmelz ist das härteste Gewebe des Körpers; es totes Material und enthält keine Nerven. Die Dicke und Dichte des Schmelzes variiert auf der Oberfläche des Zahns.

A variety of copal resin that is hard and found on the island of Zanzibar and areas of nearby African mainland. It is derived from either semi-fossilized specimens or as exudations on species Hymenaea verrucosa. The most valued pieces are fossilized specimens found embedded in the earth over a wide belt of East Africa on the western coast of Zanzibar, on tracts where not a tree is now visible. Zanzibar copal is a very hard, smooth, and shiny resin that is used to make a dark oil varnish, for fumigation, and in perfumery. Some specimens of Zanzibar copal are misrepresented as amber. Bezieht sich auf verkohlte Holzstücke, die im Kontext der Kunst im Allgemeinen als Zeichenmaterial verwendet werden. Aus möglichst homogenem Holz hergestellte Holzkohle ergibt einen porösen und wenig haftenden Strich. Zeichenkohle wird auch zu spitzen Kohlestiften verarbeitet, um dünne Linien zu erzeugen, breitflächig eingesetzt, um gleichmäßig getönte Flächen zu erzeugen, und durch Reiben und Zermahlen aufgetragen, um gedämpfte Zwischentöne und weiche Übergänge zu erzeugen.
Haar oder Haar inklusive der Haut einer Ziege, einem vierbeiniger Wiederkäuer der Gattung Capra.
A form of concrete which acts as an insulator.

A laminated leaf, resembling gold leaf, was made by placing a thin sheet of gold over a similar sheet of silver (or tin) and hammering the two together. The gold-silver laminate was used instead of gold leaf, but it had the undesirable characteristic of tarnishing, since the thin gold layer cannot protect the silver below.
